'''Tsukinami no Matsuri''' is one of the {{ill|Shinto|8=en}} {{ill|Festival|lt=festivals|8=en|12=draft}} . At {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Shrine|8=en|12=draft}}, the three festivals held in {{ill|June|8=en}} and {{ill|December|8=en|12=draft}} and the {{ill|10=ja|11=神嘗祭|Kannamesai Festival|8=en|12=draft}} are called the Sansetsu Festival and Sanjisai Festival.
Tsukinami-no-Matsuri, meaning "monthly festival," is a significant Shinto ritual traditionally observed at the Ise Grand Shrine in Japan. Despite its name, the festival is held biannually, on the 11th day of June and December. It is one of the Sansetsu-sai festivals, along with the Kanname-sai festival in October, collectively known as the three major festivals of Ise Shrine. <ref name=":0">https://www.japanesewiki.com/Shinto/Tsukinami-no-Matsuri.html</ref>
Historically, during Tsukinami-no-Matsuri, the Imperial Court would present offerings, known as heihaku (silk offerings), to 304 deities worshipped in the Kinai region. This practice, referred to as hanhei, was later conducted exclusively by the Ise Grand Shrine. Although hanhei was discontinued during the Muromachi period due to conflicts like the Ōnin War, it was reinstated in the Meiji period. <ref name=":0" />
The festival involves solemn rituals and ceremonies conducted by Shinto priests, who offer prayers for peace and prosperity. These rituals are performed under the direction of the Emperor, considered a direct descendant of Amaterasu-Omikami, the sun goddess enshrined at Ise. The ceremonies include the dedication of textiles and sacred dances known as kagura. <ref>https://www.jinjahoncho.or.jp/en/shinto/index6.html</ref>
Today, many shrines across Japan observe Tsukinami-no-Matsuri by designating a specific day each month to conduct the festival, maintaining the tradition of offering prayers and performing rituals to honor the deities and seek their blessings. <ref name=":0" />
== history == It seems that in the past it was held every month, but {{ill|Engishiki|the Engishiki|8=en|12=draft}} stipulates that it should be held on the 11th of June and December, and it was held at {{ill|10=ja|11=朝廷_(日本)|Imperial Court in Kyoto|lt=the Imperial Court|8=en|12=draft}} and at Ise Shrine.
On the morning of the 11th day, {{ill|Department of Divinities|lt=the Shinto priests|8=en|12=draft}} at the Imperial Court distributed {{ill|10=ja|11=幣帛|hanpei|lt=offerings|8=en|12=draft}} (hanpei) to the 304 {{ill|10=ja|11=祝_(神職)|Deity|lt=deities|8=en|12=draft}} of {{ill|Kansai region|the Kinai region|8=en|12=draft}} <ref name="yougo">{{Cite book|title=日本史B用語集―A併記|last=全国歴史教育研究協議会|publisher={{ill|山川出版社|8=en}}|isbn=9784634013025|edition=改訂版|series=|author-link=|access-date=|和書|12=draft}}</ref> . In the evening, a ceremony called "jinkonjiki" was held at the Shinkaden Hall of {{ill|10=ja|11=中和院|Chukain Temple|8=en|12=draft}}, in which the emperor and the gods ate grains from the previous year (old grains) <ref name="yougo" /> .
After that, handicraft offerings were only practiced at Ise Shrine, and during {{ill|Muromachi period||8=en|lt=the Muromachi period,}} handicraft offerings were abolished due to events such as {{ill|Ōnin War|lt=the Onin War|8=en|12=draft}}, but were revived after {{ill|Meiji period|lt=the Meiji period|8=en|12=draft}} . Currently, many shrines across the country hold monthly festivals on specific days each month. == Shinto Shrine of Shusse Inari in America == The Shinto Shrine of Shusse Inari in America, based in Los Angeles, holds monthly Tsukinami-sai (月次祭) ceremonies to honor and express gratitude to the nature spirits, or Kami-sama. These ceremonies are typically live-streamed on their official YouTube channel, "ShintoInari," allowing participants from various locations to join. <ref>https://shintoinari.org/202412-tsukinami/</ref>
For December 2024, the Tsukinami-sai was held on Sunday, December 1st, at 7 PM Pacific Time. The ceremony included expressions of respect, gratitude, and prayers for blessings from the Kami-sama, with a closing talk titled "Living with Kami-sama in Daily Life." The shrine encourages participants to follow traditional customs, such as offering Osaisen (donations), which support the continuation of these ceremonies and their goal of establishing a physical shrine in Los Angeles. <ref>https://shintoinari.org/202412-tsukinami/</ref>
Looking ahead, the shrine has scheduled the Saitan-sai (歳旦祭), a New Year's ceremony, on January 1st, 2024, at 11:00 AM Pacific Time. This event will be held at a pop-up shrine in Little Tokyo's Weller Court, Los Angeles, and will also be live-streamed for online participants. Following this, the first Tsukinami-sai of 2024 is planned for the same day at 3:00 PM Pacific Time, both in-person and online. <ref>https://shintoinari.org/2024saitan-202401tsukinami/</ref>
For the most current information on upcoming Tsukinami-sai ceremonies and other events, it's advisable to visit the Shinto Shrine of Shusse Inari in America's official website or their YouTube channel. <ref>https://www.youtube.com/%40shusseinarishrineofamerica1913</ref>

Currently, many shrines across the country hold monthly festivals on specific days each month. == Shinto Shrine of Shusse Inari in America == The Shinto Shrine of Shusse Inari in America, based in Los Angeles, holds monthly Tsukinami-sai (月次祭) ceremonies to honor and express gratitude to the nature spirits, or Kami-sama. These ceremonies are typically live-streamed on their official YouTube channel, "ShintoInari," allowing participants from various locations to join. <ref>https://shintoinari.org/202412-tsukinami/</ref>
For December 2024, the Tsukinami-sai was held on Sunday, December 1st, at 7 PM Pacific Time. The ceremony included expressions of respect, gratitude, and prayers for blessings from the Kami-sama, with a closing talk titled "Living with Kami-sama in Daily Life." The shrine encourages participants to follow traditional customs, such as offering Osaisen (donations), which support the continuation of these ceremonies and their goal of establishing a physical shrine in Los Angeles. <ref>https://shintoinari.org/202412-tsukinami/</ref>
Looking ahead, the shrine has scheduled the Saitan-sai (歳旦祭), a New Year's ceremony, on January 1st, 2024, at 11:00 AM Pacific Time. This event will be held at a pop-up shrine in Little Tokyo's Weller Court, Los Angeles, and will also be live-streamed for online participants. Following this, the first Tsukinami-sai of 2024 is planned for the same day at 3:00 PM Pacific Time, both in-person and online. <ref>https://shintoinari.org/2024saitan-202401tsukinami/</ref>
For the most current information on upcoming Tsukinami-sai ceremonies and other events, it's advisable to visit the Shinto Shrine of Shusse Inari in America's official website or their YouTube channel. <ref>https://www.youtube.com/%40shusseinarishrineofamerica1913</ref>

'''Tsukinami no Matsuri''' is one of the {{ill|Shinto|8=en}} {{ill|Festival|lt=festivals|8=en|12=draft}} . At {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Shrine|8=en|12=draft}}, the three festivals held in {{ill|June|8=en}} and {{ill|December|8=en|12=draft}} and the {{ill|10=ja|11=神嘗祭|Kannamesai Festival|8=en|12=draft}} are called the Sansetsu Festival and Sanjisai Festival.
Tsukinami-no-Matsuri, meaning "monthly festival," is a significant Shinto ritual traditionally observed at the Ise Grand Shrine in Japan. Despite its name, the festival is held biannually, on the 11th day of June and December. It is one of the Sansetsu-sai festivals, along with the Kanname-sai festival in October, collectively known as the three major festivals of Ise Shrine. <ref name=":0">https://www.japanesewiki.com/Shinto/Tsukinami-no-Matsuri.html</ref>
Historically, during Tsukinami-no-Matsuri, the Imperial Court would present offerings, known as heihaku (silk offerings), to 304 deities worshipped in the Kinai region. This practice, referred to as hanhei, was later conducted exclusively by the Ise Grand Shrine. Although hanhei was discontinued during the Muromachi period due to conflicts like the Ōnin War, it was reinstated in the Meiji period. <ref name=":0" />
The festival involves solemn rituals and ceremonies conducted by Shinto priests, who offer prayers for peace and prosperity. These rituals are performed under the direction of the Emperor, considered a direct descendant of Amaterasu-Omikami, the sun goddess enshrined at Ise. The ceremonies include the dedication of textiles and sacred dances known as kagura. <ref>https://www.jinjahoncho.or.jp/en/shinto/index6.html</ref>
Today, many shrines across Japan observe Tsukinami-no-Matsuri by designating a specific day each month to conduct the festival, maintaining the tradition of offering prayers and performing rituals to honor the deities and seek their blessings. <ref name=":0" />
== history == It seems that in the past it was held every month, but {{ill|Engishiki|the Engishiki|8=en|12=draft}} stipulates that it should be held on the 11th of June and December, and it was held at {{ill|10=ja|11=朝廷_(日本)|Imperial Court in Kyoto|lt=the Imperial Court|8=en|12=draft}} and at Ise Shrine.
On the morning of the 11th day, {{ill|Department of Divinities|lt=the Shinto priests|8=en|12=draft}} at the Imperial Court distributed {{ill|10=ja|11=幣帛|hanpei|lt=offerings|8=en|12=draft}} (hanpei) to the 304 {{ill|10=ja|11=祝_(神職)|Deity|lt=deities|8=en|12=draft}} of {{ill|Kansai region|the Kinai region|8=en|12=draft}} <ref name="yougo">{{Cite book|title=日本史B用語集―A併記|last=全国歴史教育研究協議会|publisher={{ill|山川出版社|8=en}}|isbn=9784634013025|edition=改訂版|series=|author-link=|access-date=|和書|12=draft}}</ref> . In the evening, a ceremony called "jinkonjiki" was held at the Shinkaden Hall of {{ill|10=ja|11=中和院|Chukain Temple|8=en|12=draft}}, in which the emperor and the gods ate grains from the previous year (old grains) <ref name="yougo" /> .
After that, handicraft offerings were only practiced at Ise Shrine, and during {{ill|Muromachi period||8=en|lt=the Muromachi period,}} handicraft offerings were abolished due to events such as {{ill|Ōnin War|lt=the Onin War|8=en|12=draft}}, but were revived after {{ill|Meiji period|lt=the Meiji period|8=en|12=draft}} . Currently, many shrines across the country hold monthly festivals on specific days each month. == Shinto Shrine of Shusse Inari in America == The Shinto Shrine of Shusse Inari in America, based in Los Angeles, holds monthly Tsukinami-sai (月次祭) ceremonies to honor and express gratitude to the nature spirits, or Kami-sama. These ceremonies are typically live-streamed on their official YouTube channel, "ShintoInari," allowing participants from various locations to join. <ref>https://shintoinari.org/202412-tsukinami/</ref>
For December 2024, the Tsukinami-sai was held on Sunday, December 1st, at 7 PM Pacific Time. The ceremony included expressions of respect, gratitude, and prayers for blessings from the Kami-sama, with a closing talk titled "Living with Kami-sama in Daily Life." The shrine encourages participants to follow traditional customs, such as offering Osaisen (donations), which support the continuation of these ceremonies and their goal of establishing a physical shrine in Los Angeles. <ref>https://shintoinari.org/202412-tsukinami/</ref>
Looking ahead, the shrine has scheduled the Saitan-sai (歳旦祭), a New Year's ceremony, on January 1st, 2024, at 11:00 AM Pacific Time. This event will be held at a pop-up shrine in Little Tokyo's Weller Court, Los Angeles, and will also be live-streamed for online participants. Following this, the first Tsukinami-sai of 2024 is planned for the same day at 3:00 PM Pacific Time, both in-person and online. <ref>https://shintoinari.org/2024saitan-202401tsukinami/</ref>
For the most current information on upcoming Tsukinami-sai ceremonies and other events, it's advisable to visit the Shinto Shrine of Shusse Inari in America's official website or their YouTube channel. <ref>https://www.youtube.com/%40shusseinarishrineofamerica1913</ref>

revision.text:
{{出典の明記|date=2017年8月28日 (月) 08:01 (UTC)}} {{令制祭祀}} '''月次祭'''（つきなみのまつり）は、[[神道]]における[[祭礼]]のひとつ。[[伊勢神宮]]では[[6月]]・[[12月]]の月次祭と[[神嘗祭]]の3つの祭礼を三節祭（さんせつさい）、三時祭（さんじさい）と称される。
==歴史== 古くは毎月行われたようであるが、『[[延喜式]]』において6月と12月の11日に行うことが規定され、[[朝廷 (日本)|朝廷]]と伊勢神宮で行われた。
朝廷では、[[神祇官]]が11日の朝に、[[畿内]]304座の神の[[祝部]]（ほうりべ、{{smaller|[[旧仮名遣]]：}}はふりべ）に[[幣帛]]を分け与えた（班幣）<ref name="yougo">{{Cite book|和書|author=全国歴史教育研究協議会|authorlink=|title=日本史B用語集―A併記|origdate=2009-3-30|accessdate=|edition=改訂版|publisher=[[山川出版社]]|series=|isbn=9784634013025}}</ref>。また、夜には[[中和院]]（ちゅうかいん）の神嘉殿で、前年に収穫した穀物（旧穀）を天皇が神と一緒に食する「神今食」（じんこんじき）が行われた<ref name="yougo" />。
その後、班幣は伊勢神宮のみとなり、[[室町時代]]に入ると[[応仁の乱]]などにより班幣は廃されるようになったが、[[明治]]以降に復活した。なお現在では、全国の多くの神社でも毎月一定の日を決めて月次祭が行われている。
==脚注== <references />
== 参考文献 == * {{Cite book|和書|author=全国歴史教育研究協議会|authorlink=|title=日本史B用語集―A併記|origdate=2009-3-30|accessdate=|edition=改訂版|publisher=[[山川出版社]]|series=|isbn=9784634013025}}
==関連項目== *[[大祭]] *[[例祭]] *[[新嘗祭]] *[[神嘗祭]] *[[小忌衣]]
{{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:つきなみのまつり}}
[[Category:神道行事]] [[Category:日本の祭り]]

Looking ahead, the shrine has scheduled the Saitan-sai (歳旦祭), a New Year's ceremony, on January 1st, 2024, at 11:00 AM Pacific Time. This event will be held at a pop-up shrine in Little Tokyo's Weller Court, Los Angeles, and will also be live-streamed for online participants. Following this, the first Tsukinami-sai of 2024 is planned for the same day at 3:00 PM Pacific Time, both in-person and online. <ref>https://shintoinari.org/2024saitan-202401tsukinami/</ref>
For the most current information on upcoming Tsukinami-sai ceremonies and other events, it's advisable to visit the Shinto Shrine of Shusse Inari in America's official website or their YouTube channel. <ref>https://www.youtube.com/%40shusseinarishrineofamerica1913</ref>

{{User:Immanuelle/Ritual ceremonies of the Imperial Palace updated}}
'''Tsukinami no Matsuri''' is one of the {{ill|Shinto|8=en}} {{ill|Festival|lt=festivals|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft}} . At {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Shrine|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the three festivals held in {{ill|June|8=en}} and {{ill|December|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and the {{ill|10=ja|11=神嘗祭|Kannamesai Festival|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft}} are called the Sansetsu Festival and Sanjisai Festival.
Tsukinami-no-Matsuri, meaning "monthly festival," is a significant Shinto ritual traditionally observed at the Ise Grand Shrine in Japan. Despite its name, the festival is held biannually, on the 11th day of June and December. It is one of the Sansetsu-sai festivals, along with the Kanname-sai festival in October, collectively known as the three major festivals of Ise Shrine. <ref name=":0">https://www.japanesewiki.com/Shinto/Tsukinami-no-Matsuri.html</ref>
Historically, during Tsukinami-no-Matsuri, the Imperial Court would present offerings, known as heihaku (silk offerings), to 304 deities worshipped in the Kinai region. This practice, referred to as hanhei, was later conducted exclusively by the Ise Grand Shrine. Although hanhei was discontinued during the Muromachi period due to conflicts like the Ōnin War, it was reinstated in the Meiji period. <ref name=":0" />
The festival involves solemn rituals and ceremonies conducted by Shinto priests, who offer prayers for peace and prosperity. These rituals are performed under the direction of the Emperor, considered a direct descendant of Amaterasu-Omikami, the sun goddess enshrined at Ise. The ceremonies include the dedication of textiles and sacred dances known as kagura. <ref>https://www.jinjahoncho.or.jp/en/shinto/index6.html</ref>
Today, many shrines across Japan observe Tsukinami-no-Matsuri by designating a specific day each month to conduct the festival, maintaining the tradition of offering prayers and performing rituals to honor the deities and seek their blessings. <ref name=":0" />
== history == It seems that in the past it was held every month, but {{ill|Engishiki|the Engishiki|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft}} stipulates that it should be held on the 11th of June and December, and it was held at {{ill|10=ja|11=朝廷_(日本)|Imperial Court in Kyoto|lt=the Imperial Court|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and at Ise Shrine.
On the morning of the 11th day, {{ill|Department of Divinities|lt=the Shinto priests|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft}} at the Imperial Court distributed {{ill|10=ja|11=幣帛|hanpei|lt=offerings|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (hanpei) to the 304 {{ill|10=ja|11=祝_(神職)|Deity|lt=deities|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft}} of {{ill|Kansai region|the Kinai region|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft}} <ref name="yougo">{{Cite book|title=日本史B用語集―A併記|last=全国歴史教育研究協議会|publisher={{ill|山川出版社|8=en}}|isbn=9784634013025|edition=改訂版|series=|author-link=|access-date=|和書|qq=draft|qq=draft}}</ref> . In the evening, a ceremony called "jinkonjiki" was held at the Shinkaden Hall of {{ill|10=ja|11=中和院|Chukain Temple|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, in which the emperor and the gods ate grains from the previous year (old grains) <ref name="yougo" /> .
After that, handicraft offerings were only practiced at Ise Shrine, and during {{ill|Muromachi period||8=en|lt=the Muromachi period,}} handicraft offerings were abolished due to events such as {{ill|Ōnin War|lt=the Onin War|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, but were revived after {{ill|Meiji period|lt=the Meiji period|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft}} . Currently, many shrines across the country hold monthly festivals on specific days each month. == Shinto Shrine of Shusse Inari in America == The Shinto Shrine of Shusse Inari in America, based in Los Angeles, holds monthly Tsukinami-sai (月次祭) ceremonies to honor and express gratitude to the nature spirits, or Kami-sama. These ceremonies are typically live-streamed on their official YouTube channel, "ShintoInari," allowing participants from various locations to join. <ref>https://shintoinari.org/202412-tsukinami/</ref>
For December 2024, the Tsukinami-sai was held on Sunday, December 1st, at 7 PM Pacific Time. The ceremony included expressions of respect, gratitude, and prayers for blessings from the Kami-sama, with a closing talk titled "Living with Kami-sama in Daily Life." The shrine encourages participants to follow traditional customs, such as offering Osaisen (donations), which support the continuation of these ceremonies and their goal of establishing a physical shrine in Los Angeles. <ref>https://shintoinari.org/202412-tsukinami/</ref>
Looking ahead, the shrine has scheduled the Saitan-sai (歳旦祭), a New Year's ceremony, on January 1st, 2024, at 11:00 AM Pacific Time. This event will be held at a pop-up shrine in Little Tokyo's Weller Court, Los Angeles, and will also be live-streamed for online participants. Following this, the first Tsukinami-sai of 2024 is planned for the same day at 3:00 PM Pacific Time, both in-person and online. <ref>https://shintoinari.org/2024saitan-202401tsukinami/</ref>
For the most current information on upcoming Tsukinami-sai ceremonies and other events, it's advisable to visit the Shinto Shrine of Shusse Inari in America's official website or their YouTube channel. <ref>https://www.youtube.com/%40shusseinarishrineofamerica1913</ref>

The ceremonies include the dedication of textiles and sacred dances known as kagura. <ref>https://www.jinjahoncho.or.jp/en/shinto/index6.html</ref>
Today, many shrines across Japan observe Tsukinami-no-Matsuri by designating a specific day each month to conduct the festival, maintaining the tradition of offering prayers and performing rituals to honor the deities and seek their blessings. <ref name=":0" />
== history == It seems that in the past it was held every month, but {{ill|Engishiki|the Engishiki|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft}} stipulates that it should be held on the 11th of June and December, and it was held at {{ill|10=ja|11=朝廷_(日本)|Imperial Court in Kyoto|lt=the Imperial Court|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and at Ise Shrine.
On the morning of the 11th day, {{ill|Department of Divinities|lt=the Shinto priests|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft}} at the Imperial Court distributed {{ill|10=ja|11=幣帛|hanpei|lt=offerings|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (hanpei) to the 304 {{ill|10=ja|11=祝_(神職)|Deity|lt=deities|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft}} of {{ill|Kansai region|the Kinai region|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft}} <ref name="yougo">{{Cite book|title=日本史B用語集―A併記|last=全国歴史教育研究協議会|publisher={{ill|山川出版社|8=en}}|isbn=9784634013025|edition=改訂版|series=|author-link=|access-date=|和書|qq=draft|qq=draft}}</ref> . In the evening, a ceremony called "jinkonjiki" was held at the Shinkaden Hall of {{ill|10=ja|11=中和院|Chukain Temple|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, in which the emperor and the gods ate grains from the previous year (old grains) <ref name="yougo" /> .
After that, handicraft offerings were only practiced at Ise Shrine, and during {{ill|Muromachi period||8=en|lt=the Muromachi period,}} handicraft offerings were abolished due to events such as {{ill|Ōnin War|lt=the Onin War|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, but were revived after {{ill|Meiji period|lt=the Meiji period|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft}} . Currently, many shrines across the country hold monthly festivals on specific days each month. == Shinto Shrine of Shusse Inari in America == The Shinto Shrine of Shusse Inari in America, based in Los Angeles, holds monthly Tsukinami-sai (月次祭) ceremonies to honor and express gratitude to the nature spirits, or Kami-sama. These ceremonies are typically live-streamed on their official YouTube channel, "ShintoInari," allowing participants from various locations to join. <ref>https://shintoinari.org/202412-tsukinami/</ref>
For December 2024, the Tsukinami-sai was held on Sunday, December 1st, at 7 PM Pacific Time. The ceremony included expressions of respect, gratitude, and prayers for blessings from the Kami-sama, with a closing talk titled "Living with Kami-sama in Daily Life." The shrine encourages participants to follow traditional customs, such as offering Osaisen (donations), which support the continuation of these ceremonies and their goal of establishing a physical shrine in Los Angeles. <ref>https://shintoinari.org/202412-tsukinami/</ref>
Looking ahead, the shrine has scheduled the Saitan-sai (歳旦祭), a New Year's ceremony, on January 1st, 2024, at 11:00 AM Pacific Time. This event will be held at a pop-up shrine in Little Tokyo's Weller Court, Los Angeles, and will also be live-streamed for online participants. Following this, the first Tsukinami-sai of 2024 is planned for the same day at 3:00 PM Pacific Time, both in-person and online. <ref>https://shintoinari.org/2024saitan-202401tsukinami/</ref>
For the most current information on upcoming Tsukinami-sai ceremonies and other events, it's advisable to visit the Shinto Shrine of Shusse Inari in America's official website or their YouTube channel. <ref>https://www.youtube.com/%40shusseinarishrineofamerica1913</ref>

'''Tsukinami no Matsuri''' is one of the {{ill|Shinto|8=en}} {{ill|Festival|lt=festivals|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft}} . At {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Shrine|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the three festivals held in {{ill|June|8=en}} and {{ill|December|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and the {{ill|10=ja|11=神嘗祭|Kannamesai Festival|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft}} are called the Sansetsu Festival and Sanjisai Festival.
Tsukinami-no-Matsuri, meaning "monthly festival," is a significant Shinto ritual traditionally observed at the Ise Grand Shrine in Japan. Despite its name, the festival is held biannually, on the 11th day of June and December. It is one of the Sansetsu-sai festivals, along with the Kanname-sai festival in October, collectively known as the three major festivals of Ise Shrine. <ref name=":0">https://www.japanesewiki.com/Shinto/Tsukinami-no-Matsuri.html</ref>
Historically, during Tsukinami-no-Matsuri, the Imperial Court would present offerings, known as heihaku (silk offerings), to 304 deities worshipped in the Kinai region. This practice, referred to as hanhei, was later conducted exclusively by the Ise Grand Shrine. Although hanhei was discontinued during the Muromachi period due to conflicts like the Ōnin War, it was reinstated in the Meiji period. <ref name=":0" />
The festival involves solemn rituals and ceremonies conducted by Shinto priests, who offer prayers for peace and prosperity. These rituals are performed under the direction of the Emperor, considered a direct descendant of Amaterasu-Omikami, the sun goddess enshrined at Ise. The ceremonies include the dedication of textiles and sacred dances known as kagura. <ref>https://www.jinjahoncho.or.jp/en/shinto/index6.html</ref>
Today, many shrines across Japan observe Tsukinami-no-Matsuri by designating a specific day each month to conduct the festival, maintaining the tradition of offering prayers and performing rituals to honor the deities and seek their blessings. <ref name=":0" />
== history == It seems that in the past it was held every month, but {{ill|Engishiki|the Engishiki|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft}} stipulates that it should be held on the 11th of June and December, and it was held at {{ill|10=ja|11=朝廷_(日本)|Imperial Court in Kyoto|lt=the Imperial Court|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and at Ise Shrine.
On the morning of the 11th day, {{ill|Department of Divinities|lt=the Shinto priests|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft}} at the Imperial Court distributed {{ill|10=ja|11=幣帛|hanpei|lt=offerings|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (hanpei) to the 304 {{ill|10=ja|11=祝_(神職)|Deity|lt=deities|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft}} of {{ill|Kansai region|the Kinai region|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft}} <ref name="yougo">{{Cite book|title=日本史B用語集―A併記|last=全国歴史教育研究協議会|publisher={{ill|山川出版社|8=en}}|isbn=9784634013025|edition=改訂版|series=|author-link=|access-date=|和書|qq=draft|qq=draft}}</ref> . In the evening, a ceremony called "jinkonjiki" was held at the Shinkaden Hall of {{ill|10=ja|11=中和院|Chukain Temple|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, in which the emperor and the gods ate grains from the previous year (old grains) <ref name="yougo" /> .
After that, handicraft offerings were only practiced at Ise Shrine, and during {{ill|Muromachi period||8=en|lt=the Muromachi period,}} handicraft offerings were abolished due to events such as {{ill|Ōnin War|lt=the Onin War|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, but were revived after {{ill|Meiji period|lt=the Meiji period|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft}} . Currently, many shrines across the country hold monthly festivals on specific days each month. == Shinto Shrine of Shusse Inari in America == The Shinto Shrine of Shusse Inari in America, based in Los Angeles, holds monthly Tsukinami-sai (月次祭) ceremonies to honor and express gratitude to the nature spirits, or Kami-sama. These ceremonies are typically live-streamed on their official YouTube channel, "ShintoInari," allowing participants from various locations to join. <ref>https://shintoinari.org/202412-tsukinami/</ref>
For December 2024, the Tsukinami-sai was held on Sunday, December 1st, at 7 PM Pacific Time. The ceremony included expressions of respect, gratitude, and prayers for blessings from the Kami-sama, with a closing talk titled "Living with Kami-sama in Daily Life." The shrine encourages participants to follow traditional customs, such as offering Osaisen (donations), which support the continuation of these ceremonies and their goal of establishing a physical shrine in Los Angeles. <ref>https://shintoinari.org/202412-tsukinami/</ref>
Looking ahead, the shrine has scheduled the Saitan-sai (歳旦祭), a New Year's ceremony, on January 1st, 2024, at 11:00 AM Pacific Time. This event will be held at a pop-up shrine in Little Tokyo's Weller Court, Los Angeles, and will also be live-streamed for online participants. Following this, the first Tsukinami-sai of 2024 is planned for the same day at 3:00 PM Pacific Time, both in-person and online. <ref>https://shintoinari.org/2024saitan-202401tsukinami/</ref>
For the most current information on upcoming Tsukinami-sai ceremonies and other events, it's advisable to visit the Shinto Shrine of Shusse Inari in America's official website or their YouTube channel. <ref>https://www.youtube.com/%40shusseinarishrineofamerica1913</ref>

'''Tsukinami no Matsuri''' is one of the {{ill|Shinto|8=en}} {{ill|Festival|lt=festivals|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft}} . At {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Shrine|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the three festivals held in {{ill|June|8=en}} and {{ill|December|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and the {{ill|10=ja|11=神嘗祭|Kannamesai Festival|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft}} are called the Sansetsu Festival and Sanjisai Festival.
Tsukinami-no-Matsuri, meaning "monthly festival," is a significant Shinto ritual traditionally observed at the Ise Grand Shrine in Japan. Despite its name, the festival is held biannually, on the 11th day of June and December. It is one of the Sansetsu-sai festivals, along with the Kanname-sai festival in October, collectively known as the three major festivals of Ise Shrine. <ref name=":0">https://www.japanesewiki.com/Shinto/Tsukinami-no-Matsuri.html</ref>
Historically, during Tsukinami-no-Matsuri, the Imperial Court would present offerings, known as heihaku (silk offerings), to 304 deities worshipped in the Kinai region. This practice, referred to as hanhei, was later conducted exclusively by the Ise Grand Shrine. Although hanhei was discontinued during the Muromachi period due to conflicts like the Ōnin War, it was reinstated in the Meiji period. <ref name=":0" />
The festival involves solemn rituals and ceremonies conducted by Shinto priests, who offer prayers for peace and prosperity. These rituals are performed under the direction of the Emperor, considered a direct descendant of Amaterasu-Omikami, the sun goddess enshrined at Ise. The ceremonies include the dedication of textiles and sacred dances known as kagura. <ref>https://www.jinjahoncho.or.jp/en/shinto/index6.html</ref>
Today, many shrines across Japan observe Tsukinami-no-Matsuri by designating a specific day each month to conduct the festival, maintaining the tradition of offering prayers and performing rituals to honor the deities and seek their blessings. <ref name=":0" />
== history == It seems that in the past it was held every month, but {{ill|Engishiki|the Engishiki|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft}} stipulates that it should be held on the 11th of June and December, and it was held at {{ill|10=ja|11=朝廷_(日本)|Imperial Court in Kyoto|lt=the Imperial Court|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and at Ise Shrine.
On the morning of the 11th day, {{ill|Department of Divinities|lt=the Shinto priests|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft}} at the Imperial Court distributed {{ill|10=ja|11=幣帛|hanpei|lt=offerings|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (hanpei) to the 304 {{ill|10=ja|11=祝_(神職)|Deity|lt=deities|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft}} of {{ill|Kansai region|the Kinai region|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft}} <ref name="yougo">{{Cite book|title=日本史B用語集―A併記|last=全国歴史教育研究協議会|publisher={{ill|山川出版社|8=en}}|isbn=9784634013025|edition=改訂版|series=|author-link=|access-date=|和書|qq=draft|qq=draft}}</ref> . In the evening, a ceremony called "jinkonjiki" was held at the Shinkaden Hall of {{ill|10=ja|11=中和院|Chukain Temple|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, in which the emperor and the gods ate grains from the previous year (old grains) <ref name="yougo" /> .
After that, handicraft offerings were only practiced at Ise Shrine, and during {{ill|Muromachi period||8=en|lt=the Muromachi period,}} handicraft offerings were abolished due to events such as {{ill|Ōnin War|lt=the Onin War|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, but were revived after {{ill|Meiji period|lt=the Meiji period|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft}} . Currently, many shrines across the country hold monthly festivals on specific days each month. == Shinto Shrine of Shusse Inari in America == The Shinto Shrine of Shusse Inari in America, based in Los Angeles, holds monthly Tsukinami-sai (月次祭) ceremonies to honor and express gratitude to the nature spirits, or Kami-sama. These ceremonies are typically live-streamed on their official YouTube channel, "ShintoInari," allowing participants from various locations to join. <ref>https://shintoinari.org/202412-tsukinami/</ref>
For December 2024, the Tsukinami-sai was held on Sunday, December 1st, at 7 PM Pacific Time. The ceremony included expressions of respect, gratitude, and prayers for blessings from the Kami-sama, with a closing talk titled "Living with Kami-sama in Daily Life." The shrine encourages participants to follow traditional customs, such as offering Osaisen (donations), which support the continuation of these ceremonies and their goal of establishing a physical shrine in Los Angeles. <ref>https://shintoinari.org/202412-tsukinami/</ref>
Looking ahead, the shrine has scheduled the Saitan-sai (歳旦祭), a New Year's ceremony, on January 1st, 2024, at 11:00 AM Pacific Time. This event will be held at a pop-up shrine in Little Tokyo's Weller Court, Los Angeles, and will also be live-streamed for online participants. Following this, the first Tsukinami-sai of 2024 is planned for the same day at 3:00 PM Pacific Time, both in-person and online. <ref>https://shintoinari.org/2024saitan-202401tsukinami/</ref>
For the most current information on upcoming Tsukinami-sai ceremonies and other events, it's advisable to visit the Shinto Shrine of Shusse Inari in America's official website or their YouTube channel. <ref>https://www.youtube.com/%40shusseinarishrineofamerica1913</ref>

== history == It seems that in the past it was held every month, but {{ill|Engishiki|the Engishiki|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki|qq=}} stipulates that it should be held on the 11th of June and December, and it was held at {{ill|10=ja|11=朝廷_(日本)|Imperial Court in Kyoto|lt=the Imperial Court|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} and at Ise Shrine.
On the morning of the 11th day, {{ill|Department of Divinities|lt=the Shinto priests|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Department of Divinities|qq=}} at the Imperial Court distributed {{ill|10=ja|11=幣帛|hanpei|lt=offerings|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} (hanpei) to the 304 {{ill|10=ja|11=祝_(神職)|Kami|lt=Kami|lt=Deity|lt=deities|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} of {{ill|Kansai region|the Kinai region|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansai region|qq=}} <ref name="yougo">{{Cite book|title=日本史B用語集―A併記|last=全国歴史教育研究協議会|publisher={{ill|山川出版社|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/山川出版社|qq=}}|isbn=9784634013025|edition=改訂版|series=|author-link=|access-date=|和書|qq=draft|qq=draft}}</ref> . In the evening, a ceremony called "jinkonjiki" was held at the Shinkaden Hall of {{ill|10=ja|11=中和院|Chukain Temple|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}}, in which the emperor and the gods ate grains from the previous year (old grains) <ref name="yougo" /> .
After that, handicraft offerings were only practiced at Ise Shrine, and during {{ill|Muromachi period|8=en|lt=the Muromachi period,|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi period|qq=}} handicraft offerings were abolished due to events such as {{ill|Ōnin War|lt=the Onin War|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnin War|qq=}}, but were revived after {{ill|Meiji period|lt=the Meiji period|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji period|qq=}} . Currently, many shrines across the country hold monthly festivals on specific days each month. == Shinto Shrine of Shusse Inari in America == The Shinto Shrine of Shusse Inari in America, based in Los Angeles, holds monthly Tsukinami-sai (月次祭) ceremonies to honor and express gratitude to the nature spirits, or Kami-sama. These ceremonies are typically live-streamed on their official YouTube channel, "ShintoInari," allowing participants from various locations to join. <ref>https://shintoinari.org/202412-tsukinami/</ref>
For December 2024, the Tsukinami-sai was held on Sunday, December 1st, at 7 PM Pacific Time. The ceremony included expressions of respect, gratitude, and prayers for blessings from the Kami-sama, with a closing talk titled "Living with Kami-sama in Daily Life." The shrine encourages participants to follow traditional customs, such as offering Osaisen (donations), which support the continuation of these ceremonies and their goal of establishing a physical shrine in Los Angeles. <ref>https://shintoinari.org/202412-tsukinami/</ref>
Looking ahead, the shrine has scheduled the Saitan-sai (歳旦祭), a New Year's ceremony, on January 1st, 2024, at 11:00 AM Pacific Time. This event will be held at a pop-up shrine in Little Tokyo's Weller Court, Los Angeles, and will also be live-streamed for online participants. Following this, the first Tsukinami-sai of 2024 is planned for the same day at 3:00 PM Pacific Time, both in-person and online. <ref>https://shintoinari.org/2024saitan-202401tsukinami/</ref>
For the most current information on upcoming Tsukinami-sai ceremonies and other events, it's advisable to visit the Shinto Shrine of Shusse Inari in America's official website or their YouTube channel. <ref>https://www.youtube.com/%40shusseinarishrineofamerica1913</ref>

'''Tsukinami no Matsuri''' is one of the {{ill|Shinto|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto|qq=}} {{ill|Festival|lt=festivals|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Festival|qq=}} . At {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Shrine|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Grand Shrine|qq=}}, the three festivals held in {{ill|June|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/June|qq=}} and {{ill|December|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/December|qq=}} and the [[:en:Kannamesai Festival|Kannamesai Festival]] are called the Sansetsu Festival and Sanjisai Festival.
Tsukinami-no-Matsuri, meaning "monthly festival," is a significant Shinto ritual traditionally observed at the Ise Grand Shrine in Japan. Despite its name, the festival is held biannually, on the 11th day of June and December. It is one of the Sansetsu-sai festivals, along with the Kanname-sai festival in October, collectively known as the three major festivals of Ise Shrine. <ref name=":0">https://www.japanesewiki.com/Shinto/Tsukinami-no-Matsuri.html</ref>
Historically, during Tsukinami-no-Matsuri, the Imperial Court would present offerings, known as heihaku (silk offerings), to 304 deities worshipped in the Kinai region. This practice, referred to as hanhei, was later conducted exclusively by the Ise Grand Shrine. Although hanhei was discontinued during the Muromachi period due to conflicts like the Ōnin War, it was reinstated in the Meiji period. <ref name=":0" />
The festival involves solemn rituals and ceremonies conducted by Shinto priests, who offer prayers for peace and prosperity. These rituals are performed under the direction of the Emperor, considered a direct descendant of Amaterasu-Omikami, the sun goddess enshrined at Ise. The ceremonies include the dedication of textiles and sacred dances known as kagura. <ref>https://www.jinjahoncho.or.jp/en/shinto/index6.html</ref>
Today, many shrines across Japan observe Tsukinami-no-Matsuri by designating a specific day each month to conduct the festival, maintaining the tradition of offering prayers and performing rituals to honor the deities and seek their blessings. <ref name=":0" />
== history == It seems that in the past it was held every month, but {{ill|Engishiki|the Engishiki|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki|qq=}} stipulates that it should be held on the 11th of June and December, and it was held at {{ill|10=ja|11=朝廷_(日本)|Imperial Court in Kyoto|lt=the Imperial Court|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} and at Ise Shrine.
On the morning of the 11th day, {{ill|Department of Divinities|lt=the Shinto priests|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Department of Divinities|qq=}} at the Imperial Court distributed {{ill|10=ja|11=幣帛|hanpei|lt=offerings|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} (hanpei) to the 304 {{ill|10=ja|11=祝_(神職)|Kami|lt=Kami|lt=Deity|lt=deities|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} of {{ill|Kansai region|the Kinai region|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansai region|qq=}} <ref name="yougo">{{Cite book|title=日本史B用語集―A併記|last=全国歴史教育研究協議会|publisher={{ill|山川出版社|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/山川出版社|qq=}}|isbn=9784634013025|edition=改訂版|series=|author-link=|access-date=|和書|qq=draft|qq=draft}}</ref> . In the evening, a ceremony called "jinkonjiki" was held at the Shinkaden Hall of {{ill|10=ja|11=中和院|Chukain Temple|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}}, in which the emperor and the gods ate grains from the previous year (old grains) <ref name="yougo" /> .
After that, handicraft offerings were only practiced at Ise Shrine, and during {{ill|Muromachi period|8=en|lt=the Muromachi period,|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi period|qq=}} handicraft offerings were abolished due to events such as {{ill|Ōnin War|lt=the Onin War|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnin War|qq=}}, but were revived after {{ill|Meiji period|lt=the Meiji period|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji period|qq=}} . Currently, many shrines across the country hold monthly festivals on specific days each month. == Shinto Shrine of Shusse Inari in America == The Shinto Shrine of Shusse Inari in America, based in Los Angeles, holds monthly Tsukinami-sai (月次祭) ceremonies to honor and express gratitude to the nature spirits, or Kami-sama. These ceremonies are typically live-streamed on their official YouTube channel, "ShintoInari," allowing participants from various locations to join. <ref>https://shintoinari.org/202412-tsukinami/</ref>
For December 2024, the Tsukinami-sai was held on Sunday, December 1st, at 7 PM Pacific Time. The ceremony included expressions of respect, gratitude, and prayers for blessings from the Kami-sama, with a closing talk titled "Living with Kami-sama in Daily Life." The shrine encourages participants to follow traditional customs, such as offering Osaisen (donations), which support the continuation of these ceremonies and their goal of establishing a physical shrine in Los Angeles. <ref>https://shintoinari.org/202412-tsukinami/</ref>
Looking ahead, the shrine has scheduled the Saitan-sai (歳旦祭), a New Year's ceremony, on January 1st, 2024, at 11:00 AM Pacific Time. This event will be held at a pop-up shrine in Little Tokyo's Weller Court, Los Angeles, and will also be live-streamed for online participants. Following this, the first Tsukinami-sai of 2024 is planned for the same day at 3:00 PM Pacific Time, both in-person and online. <ref>https://shintoinari.org/2024saitan-202401tsukinami/</ref>
For the most current information on upcoming Tsukinami-sai ceremonies and other events, it's advisable to visit the Shinto Shrine of Shusse Inari in America's official website or their YouTube channel. <ref>https://www.youtube.com/%40shusseinarishrineofamerica1913</ref>

This event will be held at a pop-up shrine in Little Tokyo's Weller Court, Los Angeles, and will also be live-streamed for online participants. Following this, the first Tsukinami-sai of 2024 is planned for the same day at 3:00 PM Pacific Time, both in-person and online. <ref>https://shintoinari.org/2024saitan-202401tsukinami/</ref>
For the most current information on upcoming Tsukinami-sai ceremonies and other events, it's advisable to visit the Shinto Shrine of Shusse Inari in America's official website or their YouTube channel. <ref>https://www.youtube.com/%40shusseinarishrineofamerica1913</ref>

'''Tsukinami no Matsuri''' is one of the {{ill|Shinto|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto|qq=}} {{ill|Festival|lt=festivals|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Festival|qq=}} . At {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Shrine|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Grand Shrine|qq=}}, the three festivals held in {{ill|June|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/June|qq=}} and {{ill|December|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/December|qq=}} and the [[:en:Kannamesai Festival|Kannamesai Festival]] are called the Sansetsu Festival and Sanjisai Festival.
Tsukinami-no-Matsuri, meaning "monthly festival," is a significant Shinto ritual traditionally observed at the Ise Grand Shrine in Japan. Despite its name, the festival is held biannually, on the 11th day of June and December. It is one of the Sansetsu-sai festivals, along with the Kanname-sai festival in October, collectively known as the three major festivals of Ise Shrine. <ref name=":0">https://www.japanesewiki.com/Shinto/Tsukinami-no-Matsuri.html</ref>
Historically, during Tsukinami-no-Matsuri, the Imperial Court would present offerings, known as heihaku (silk offerings), to 304 deities worshipped in the Kinai region. This practice, referred to as hanhei, was later conducted exclusively by the Ise Grand Shrine. Although hanhei was discontinued during the Muromachi period due to conflicts like the Ōnin War, it was reinstated in the Meiji period. <ref name=":0" />
The festival involves solemn rituals and ceremonies conducted by Shinto priests, who offer prayers for peace and prosperity. These rituals are performed under the direction of the Emperor, considered a direct descendant of Amaterasu-Omikami, the sun goddess enshrined at Ise. The ceremonies include the dedication of textiles and sacred dances known as kagura. <ref>https://www.jinjahoncho.or.jp/en/shinto/index6.html</ref>
Today, many shrines across Japan observe Tsukinami-no-Matsuri by designating a specific day each month to conduct the festival, maintaining the tradition of offering prayers and performing rituals to honor the deities and seek their blessings. <ref name=":0" />
== history == It seems that in the past it was held every month, but {{ill|Engishiki|the Engishiki|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki|qq=}} stipulates that it should be held on the 11th of June and December, and it was held at {{ill|10=ja|11=朝廷_(日本)|Imperial Court in Kyoto|lt=the Imperial Court|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} and at Ise Shrine.
On the morning of the 11th day, {{ill|Department of Divinities|lt=the Shinto priests|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Department of Divinities|qq=}} at the Imperial Court distributed {{ill|10=ja|11=幣帛|hanpei|lt=offerings|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} (hanpei) to the 304 {{ill|10=ja|11=祝_(神職)|Kami|lt=Kami|lt=Deity|lt=deities|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} of {{ill|Kansai region|the Kinai region|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansai region|qq=}} <ref name="yougo">{{Cite book|title=日本史B用語集―A併記|last=全国歴史教育研究協議会|publisher={{ill|山川出版社|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/山川出版社|qq=}}|isbn=9784634013025|edition=改訂版|series=|author-link=|access-date=|和書|qq=draft|qq=draft}}</ref> . In the evening, a ceremony called "jinkonjiki" was held at the Shinkaden Hall of {{ill|10=ja|11=中和院|Chukain Temple|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}}, in which the emperor and the gods ate grains from the previous year (old grains) <ref name="yougo" /> .
After that, handicraft offerings were only practiced at Ise Shrine, and during {{ill|Muromachi period|8=en|lt=the Muromachi period,|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi period|qq=}} handicraft offerings were abolished due to events such as {{ill|Ōnin War|lt=the Onin War|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnin War|qq=}}, but were revived after {{ill|Meiji period|lt=the Meiji period|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji period|qq=}} . Currently, many shrines across the country hold monthly festivals on specific days each month. == Shinto Shrine of Shusse Inari in America == The Shinto Shrine of Shusse Inari in America, based in Los Angeles, holds monthly Tsukinami-sai (月次祭) ceremonies to honor and express gratitude to the nature spirits, or Kami-sama. These ceremonies are typically live-streamed on their official YouTube channel, "ShintoInari," allowing participants from various locations to join. <ref>https://shintoinari.org/202412-tsukinami/</ref>
For December 2024, the Tsukinami-sai was held on Sunday, December 1st, at 7 PM Pacific Time. The ceremony included expressions of respect, gratitude, and prayers for blessings from the Kami-sama, with a closing talk titled "Living with Kami-sama in Daily Life." The shrine encourages participants to follow traditional customs, such as offering Osaisen (donations), which support the continuation of these ceremonies and their goal of establishing a physical shrine in Los Angeles. <ref>https://shintoinari.org/202412-tsukinami/</ref>
Looking ahead, the shrine has scheduled the Saitan-sai (歳旦祭), a New Year's ceremony, on January 1st, 2024, at 11:00 AM Pacific Time. This event will be held at a pop-up shrine in Little Tokyo's Weller Court, Los Angeles, and will also be live-streamed for online participants. Following this, the first Tsukinami-sai of 2024 is planned for the same day at 3:00 PM Pacific Time, both in-person and online. <ref>https://shintoinari.org/2024saitan-202401tsukinami/</ref>
For the most current information on upcoming Tsukinami-sai ceremonies and other events, it's advisable to visit the Shinto Shrine of Shusse Inari in America's official website or their YouTube channel. <ref>https://www.youtube.com/%40shusseinarishrineofamerica1913</ref>

'''Tsukinami no Matsuri''' is one of the {{ill|Shinto|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto|qq=}} {{ill|Festival|lt=festivals|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Festival|qq=}} . At {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Shrine|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Grand Shrine|qq=}}, the three festivals held in {{ill|June|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/June|qq=}} and {{ill|December|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/December|qq=}} and the [[:en:Kannamesai Festival|Kannamesai Festival]] are called the Sansetsu Festival and Sanjisai Festival.
Tsukinami-no-Matsuri, meaning "monthly festival," is a significant Shinto ritual traditionally observed at the Ise Grand Shrine in Japan. Despite its name, the festival is held biannually, on the 11th day of June and December. It is one of the Sansetsu-sai festivals, along with the Kanname-sai festival in October, collectively known as the three major festivals of Ise Shrine. <ref name=":0">https://www.japanesewiki.com/Shinto/Tsukinami-no-Matsuri.html</ref>
Historically, during Tsukinami-no-Matsuri, the Imperial Court would present offerings, known as heihaku (silk offerings), to 304 deities worshipped in the Kinai region. This practice, referred to as hanhei, was later conducted exclusively by the Ise Grand Shrine. Although hanhei was discontinued during the Muromachi period due to conflicts like the Ōnin War, it was reinstated in the Meiji period. <ref name=":0" />
The festival involves solemn rituals and ceremonies conducted by Shinto priests, who offer prayers for peace and prosperity. These rituals are performed under the direction of the Emperor, considered a direct descendant of Amaterasu-Omikami, the sun goddess enshrined at Ise. The ceremonies include the dedication of textiles and sacred dances known as kagura. <ref>https://www.jinjahoncho.or.jp/en/shinto/index6.html</ref>
Today, many shrines across Japan observe Tsukinami-no-Matsuri by designating a specific day each month to conduct the festival, maintaining the tradition of offering prayers and performing rituals to honor the deities and seek their blessings. <ref name=":0" />
== history == It seems that in the past it was held every month, but {{ill|Engishiki|the Engishiki|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki|qq=}} stipulates that it should be held on the 11th of June and December, and it was held at {{ill|10=ja|11=朝廷_(日本)|Imperial Court in Kyoto|lt=the Imperial Court|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} and at Ise Shrine.
On the morning of the 11th day, {{ill|Department of Divinities|lt=the Shinto priests|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Department of Divinities|qq=}} at the Imperial Court distributed {{ill|10=ja|11=幣帛|hanpei|lt=offerings|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} (hanpei) to the 304 {{ill|10=ja|11=祝_(神職)|Kami|lt=Kami|lt=Deity|lt=deities|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} of {{ill|Kansai region|the Kinai region|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansai region|qq=}} <ref name="yougo">{{Cite book|title=日本史B用語集―A併記|last=全国歴史教育研究協議会|publisher={{ill|山川出版社|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/山川出版社|qq=}}|isbn=9784634013025|edition=改訂版|series=|author-link=|access-date=|和書|qq=draft|qq=draft}}</ref> . In the evening, a ceremony called "jinkonjiki" was held at the Shinkaden Hall of {{ill|10=ja|11=中和院|Chukain Temple|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}}, in which the emperor and the gods ate grains from the previous year (old grains) <ref name="yougo" /> .
After that, handicraft offerings were only practiced at Ise Shrine, and during {{ill|Muromachi period|8=en|lt=the Muromachi period,|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi period|qq=}} handicraft offerings were abolished due to events such as {{ill|Ōnin War|lt=the Onin War|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnin War|qq=}}, but were revived after {{ill|Meiji period|lt=the Meiji period|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji period|qq=}} . Currently, many shrines across the country hold monthly festivals on specific days each month. == Shinto Shrine of Shusse Inari in America == The Shinto Shrine of Shusse Inari in America, based in Los Angeles, holds monthly Tsukinami-sai (月次祭) ceremonies to honor and express gratitude to the nature spirits, or Kami-sama. These ceremonies are typically live-streamed on their official YouTube channel, "ShintoInari," allowing participants from various locations to join. <ref>https://shintoinari.org/202412-tsukinami/</ref>
For December 2024, the Tsukinami-sai was held on Sunday, December 1st, at 7 PM Pacific Time. The ceremony included expressions of respect, gratitude, and prayers for blessings from the Kami-sama, with a closing talk titled "Living with Kami-sama in Daily Life." The shrine encourages participants to follow traditional customs, such as offering Osaisen (donations), which support the continuation of these ceremonies and their goal of establishing a physical shrine in Los Angeles. <ref>https://shintoinari.org/202412-tsukinami/</ref>
Looking ahead, the shrine has scheduled the Saitan-sai (歳旦祭), a New Year's ceremony, on January 1st, 2024, at 11:00 AM Pacific Time. This event will be held at a pop-up shrine in Little Tokyo's Weller Court, Los Angeles, and will also be live-streamed for online participants. Following this, the first Tsukinami-sai of 2024 is planned for the same day at 3:00 PM Pacific Time, both in-person and online. <ref>https://shintoinari.org/2024saitan-202401tsukinami/</ref>
For the most current information on upcoming Tsukinami-sai ceremonies and other events, it's advisable to visit the Shinto Shrine of Shusse Inari in America's official website or their YouTube channel. <ref>https://www.youtube.com/%40shusseinarishrineofamerica1913</ref>

The ceremonies include the dedication of textiles and sacred dances known as kagura. <ref>https://www.jinjahoncho.or.jp/en/shinto/index6.html</ref>

== history == It seems that in the past it was held every month, but {{ill|Engishiki|the Engishiki|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki|qq=}} stipulates that it should be held on the 11th of June and December, and it was held at {{ill|10=ja|11=朝廷_(日本)|Imperial Court in Kyoto|lt=the Imperial Court|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} and at Ise Shrine.
On the morning of the 11th day, {{ill|Department of Divinities|lt=the Shinto priests|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Department of Divinities|qq=}} at the Imperial Court distributed {{ill|10=ja|11=幣帛|hanpei|lt=offerings|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} (hanpei) to the 304 {{ill|10=ja|11=祝_(神職)|Kami|lt=Kami|lt=Deity|lt=deities|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} of {{ill|Kansai region|the Kinai region|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansai region|qq=}} <ref name="yougo">{{Cite book|title=日本史B用語集―A併記|last=全国歴史教育研究協議会|publisher={{ill|山川出版社|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/山川出版社|qq=}}|isbn=9784634013025|edition=改訂版|series=|author-link=|access-date=|和書|qq=draft|qq=draft}}</ref> . In the evening, a ceremony called "jinkonjiki" was held at the Shinkaden Hall of {{ill|10=ja|11=中和院|Chukain Temple|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}}, in which the emperor and the gods ate grains from the previous year (old grains) <ref name="yougo" /> .
After that, handicraft offerings were only practiced at Ise Shrine, and during {{ill|Muromachi period|8=en|lt=the Muromachi period,|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi period|qq=}} handicraft offerings were abolished due to events such as {{ill|Ōnin War|lt=the Onin War|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnin War|qq=}}, but were revived after {{ill|Meiji period|lt=the Meiji period|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji period|qq=}} . Currently, many shrines across the country hold monthly festivals on specific days each month. == Shinto Shrine of Shusse Inari in America == The Shinto Shrine of Shusse Inari in America, based in Los Angeles, holds monthly Tsukinami-sai (月次祭) ceremonies to honor and express gratitude to the nature spirits, or Kami-sama. These ceremonies are typically live-streamed on their official YouTube channel, "ShintoInari," allowing participants from various locations to join. <ref>https://shintoinari.org/202412-tsukinami/</ref>
For December 2024, the Tsukinami-sai was held on Sunday, December 1st, at 7 PM Pacific Time. The ceremony included expressions of respect, gratitude, and prayers for blessings from the Kami-sama, with a closing talk titled "Living with Kami-sama in Daily Life." The shrine encourages participants to follow traditional customs, such as offering Osaisen (donations), which support the continuation of these ceremonies and their goal of establishing a physical shrine in Los Angeles. <ref>https://shintoinari.org/202412-tsukinami/</ref>
Looking ahead, the shrine has scheduled the Saitan-sai (歳旦祭), a New Year's ceremony, on January 1st, 2024, at 11:00 AM Pacific Time. This event will be held at a pop-up shrine in Little Tokyo's Weller Court, Los Angeles, and will also be live-streamed for online participants. Following this, the first Tsukinami-sai of 2024 is planned for the same day at 3:00 PM Pacific Time, both in-person and online. <ref>https://shintoinari.org/2024saitan-202401tsukinami/</ref>
For the most current information on upcoming Tsukinami-sai ceremonies and other events, it's advisable to visit the Shinto Shrine of Shusse Inari in America's official website or their YouTube channel. <ref>https://www.youtube.com/%40shusseinarishrineofamerica1913</ref>

'''Tsukinami no Matsuri''' is one of the {{ill|Shinto|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto|qq=}} {{ill|Festival|lt=festivals|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Festival|qq=}} . At {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Shrine|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Grand Shrine|qq=}}, the three festivals held in {{ill|June|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/June|qq=}} and {{ill|December|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/December|qq=}} and the [[:en:Kannamesai Festival|Kannamesai Festival]] are called the Sansetsu Festival and Sanjisai Festival.
Tsukinami-no-Matsuri, meaning "monthly festival," is a significant Shinto ritual traditionally observed at the Ise Grand Shrine in Japan. Despite its name, the festival is held biannually, on the 11th day of June and December. It is one of the Sansetsu-sai festivals, along with the Kanname-sai festival in October, collectively known as the three major festivals of Ise Shrine. <ref name=":0">https://www.japanesewiki.com/Shinto/Tsukinami-no-Matsuri.html</ref>
Historically, during Tsukinami-no-Matsuri, the Imperial Court would present offerings, known as heihaku (silk offerings), to 304 deities worshipped in the Kinai region. This practice, referred to as hanhei, was later conducted exclusively by the Ise Grand Shrine. Although hanhei was discontinued during the Muromachi period due to conflicts like the Ōnin War, it was reinstated in the Meiji period. <ref name=":0" />
The festival involves solemn rituals and ceremonies conducted by Shinto priests, who offer prayers for peace and prosperity. These rituals are performed under the direction of the Emperor, considered a direct descendant of Amaterasu-Omikami, the sun goddess enshrined at Ise. The ceremonies include the dedication of textiles and sacred dances known as kagura. <ref>https://www.jinjahoncho.or.jp/en/shinto/index6.html</ref>
Today, many shrines across Japan observe Tsukinami-no-Matsuri by designating a specific day each month to conduct the festival, maintaining the tradition of offering prayers and performing rituals to honor the deities and seek their blessings. <ref name=":0" />
== history == It seems that in the past it was held every month, but {{ill|Engishiki|the Engishiki|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki|qq=}} stipulates that it should be held on the 11th of June and December, and it was held at {{ill|10=ja|11=朝廷_(日本)|Imperial Court in Kyoto|lt=the Imperial Court|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} and at Ise Shrine.
On the morning of the 11th day, {{ill|Department of Divinities|lt=the Shinto priests|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Department of Divinities|qq=}} at the Imperial Court distributed {{ill|10=ja|11=幣帛|hanpei|lt=offerings|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} (hanpei) to the 304 {{ill|10=ja|11=祝_(神職)|Kami|lt=Kami|lt=Deity|lt=deities|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} of {{ill|Kansai region|the Kinai region|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansai region|qq=}} <ref name="yougo">{{Cite book|title=日本史B用語集―A併記|last=全国歴史教育研究協議会|publisher={{ill|山川出版社|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/山川出版社|qq=}}|isbn=9784634013025|edition=改訂版|series=|author-link=|access-date=|和書|qq=draft|qq=draft}}</ref> . In the evening, a ceremony called "jinkonjiki" was held at the Shinkaden Hall of {{ill|10=ja|11=中和院|Chukain Temple|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}}, in which the emperor and the gods ate grains from the previous year (old grains) <ref name="yougo" /> .
After that, handicraft offerings were only practiced at Ise Shrine, and during {{ill|Muromachi period|8=en|lt=the Muromachi period,|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi period|qq=}} handicraft offerings were abolished due to events such as {{ill|Ōnin War|lt=the Onin War|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnin War|qq=}}, but were revived after {{ill|Meiji period|lt=the Meiji period|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji period|qq=}} . Currently, many shrines across the country hold monthly festivals on specific days each month. == Shinto Shrine of Shusse Inari in America == The Shinto Shrine of Shusse Inari in America, based in Los Angeles, holds monthly Tsukinami-sai (月次祭) ceremonies to honor and express gratitude to the nature spirits, or Kami-sama. These ceremonies are typically live-streamed on their official YouTube channel, "ShintoInari," allowing participants from various locations to join. <ref>https://shintoinari.org/202412-tsukinami/</ref>
For December 2024, the Tsukinami-sai was held on Sunday, December 1st, at 7 PM Pacific Time. The ceremony included expressions of respect, gratitude, and prayers for blessings from the Kami-sama, with a closing talk titled "Living with Kami-sama in Daily Life." The shrine encourages participants to follow traditional customs, such as offering Osaisen (donations), which support the continuation of these ceremonies and their goal of establishing a physical shrine in Los Angeles. <ref>https://shintoinari.org/202412-tsukinami/</ref>
Looking ahead, the shrine has scheduled the Saitan-sai (歳旦祭), a New Year's ceremony, on January 1st, 2024, at 11:00 AM Pacific Time. This event will be held at a pop-up shrine in Little Tokyo's Weller Court, Los Angeles, and will also be live-streamed for online participants. Following this, the first Tsukinami-sai of 2024 is planned for the same day at 3:00 PM Pacific Time, both in-person and online. <ref>https://shintoinari.org/2024saitan-202401tsukinami/</ref>
For the most current information on upcoming Tsukinami-sai ceremonies and other events, it's advisable to visit the Shinto Shrine of Shusse Inari in America's official website or their YouTube channel. <ref>https://www.youtube.com/%40shusseinarishrineofamerica1913</ref>

The ceremony included expressions of respect, gratitude, and prayers for blessings from the Kami-sama, with a closing talk titled "Living with Kami-sama in Daily Life." The shrine encourages participants to follow traditional customs, such as offering Osaisen (donations), which support the continuation of these ceremonies and their goal of establishing a physical shrine in Los Angeles. <ref>https://shintoinari.org/202412-tsukinami/</ref>
Looking ahead, the shrine has scheduled the Saitan-sai (歳旦祭), a New Year's ceremony, on January 1st, 2024, at 11:00 AM Pacific Time. This event will be held at a pop-up shrine in Little Tokyo's Weller Court, Los Angeles, and will also be live-streamed for online participants. Following this, the first Tsukinami-sai of 2024 is planned for the same day at 3:00 PM Pacific Time, both in-person and online. <ref>https://shintoinari.org/2024saitan-202401tsukinami/</ref>
For the most current information on upcoming Tsukinami-sai ceremonies and other events, it's advisable to visit the Shinto Shrine of Shusse Inari in America's official website or their YouTube channel. <ref>https://www.youtube.com/%40shusseinarishrineofamerica1913</ref>

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11516161]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:07, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created in the draft namespace on english wikipedia. {{User:Immanuelle/Ritual ceremonies of the Imperial Palace updated}}
'''Tsukinami no Matsuri''' is one of the {{ill|Shinto|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto|qq=}} {{ill|Festival|lt=festivals|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Festival|qq=}} . At {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Shrine|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Grand Shrine|qq=}}, the three festivals held in {{ill|June|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/June|qq=}} and {{ill|December|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/December|qq=}} and the [[:en:Kannamesai Festival|Kannamesai Festival]] are called the Sansetsu Festival and Sanjisai Festival.
Tsukinami-no-Matsuri, meaning "monthly festival," is a significant Shinto ritual traditionally observed at the Ise Grand Shrine in Japan. Despite its name, the festival is held biannually, on the 11th day of June and December. It is one of the Sansetsu-sai festivals, along with the Kanname-sai festival in October, collectively known as the three major festivals of Ise Shrine. <ref name=":0">https://www.japanesewiki.com/Shinto/Tsukinami-no-Matsuri.html</ref>
Historically, during Tsukinami-no-Matsuri, the Imperial Court would present offerings, known as heihaku (silk offerings), to 304 deities worshipped in the Kinai region. This practice, referred to as hanhei, was later conducted exclusively by the Ise Grand Shrine. Although hanhei was discontinued during the Muromachi period due to conflicts like the Ōnin War, it was reinstated in the Meiji period. <ref name=":0" />
The festival involves solemn rituals and ceremonies conducted by Shinto priests, who offer prayers for peace and prosperity. These rituals are performed under the direction of the Emperor, considered a direct descendant of Amaterasu-Omikami, the sun goddess enshrined at Ise. The ceremonies include the dedication of textiles and sacred dances known as kagura. <ref>https://www.jinjahoncho.or.jp/en/shinto/index6.html</ref>
Today, many shrines across Japan observe Tsukinami-no-Matsuri by designating a specific day each month to conduct the festival, maintaining the tradition of offering prayers and performing rituals to honor the deities and seek their blessings. <ref name=":0" />
== history == It seems that in the past it was held every month, but {{ill|Engishiki|the Engishiki|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki|qq=}} stipulates that it should be held on the 11th of June and December, and it was held at {{ill|10=ja|11=朝廷_(日本)|Imperial Court in Kyoto|lt=the Imperial Court|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} and at Ise Shrine.
On the morning of the 11th day, {{ill|Department of Divinities|lt=the Shinto priests|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Department of Divinities|qq=}} at the Imperial Court distributed {{ill|10=ja|11=幣帛|hanpei|lt=offerings|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} (hanpei) to the 304 {{ill|10=ja|11=祝_(神職)|Kami|lt=Kami|lt=Deity|lt=deities|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} of {{ill|Kansai region|the Kinai region|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansai region|qq=}} <ref name="yougo">{{Cite book|title=日本史B用語集―A併記|last=全国歴史教育研究協議会|publisher={{ill|山川出版社|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/山川出版社|qq=}}|isbn=9784634013025|edition=改訂版|series=|author-link=|access-date=|和書|qq=draft|qq=draft}}</ref> . In the evening, a ceremony called "jinkonjiki" was held at the Shinkaden Hall of {{ill|10=ja|11=中和院|Chukain Temple|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}}, in which the emperor and the gods ate grains from the previous year (old grains) <ref name="yougo" /> .
After that, handicraft offerings were only practiced at Ise Shrine, and during {{ill|Muromachi period|8=en|lt=the Muromachi period,|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi period|qq=}} handicraft offerings were abolished due to events such as {{ill|Ōnin War|lt=the Onin War|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnin War|qq=}}, but were revived after {{ill|Meiji period|lt=the Meiji period|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji period|qq=}} . Currently, many shrines across the country hold monthly festivals on specific days each month. == Shinto Shrine of Shusse Inari in America == The Shinto Shrine of Shusse Inari in America, based in Los Angeles, holds monthly Tsukinami-sai (月次祭) ceremonies to honor and express gratitude to the nature spirits, or Kami-sama. These ceremonies are typically live-streamed on their official YouTube channel, "ShintoInari," allowing participants from various locations to join. <ref>https://shintoinari.org/202412-tsukinami/</ref>
For December 2024, the Tsukinami-sai was held on Sunday, December 1st, at 7 PM Pacific Time. The ceremony included expressions of respect, gratitude, and prayers for blessings from the Kami-sama, with a closing talk titled "Living with Kami-sama in Daily Life." The shrine encourages participants to follow traditional customs, such as offering Osaisen (donations), which support the continuation of these ceremonies and their goal of establishing a physical shrine in Los Angeles. <ref>https://shintoinari.org/202412-tsukinami/</ref>
Looking ahead, the shrine has scheduled the Saitan-sai (歳旦祭), a New Year's ceremony, on January 1st, 2024, at 11:00 AM Pacific Time. This event will be held at a pop-up shrine in Little Tokyo's Weller Court, Los Angeles, and will also be live-streamed for online participants. Following this, the first Tsukinami-sai of 2024 is planned for the same day at 3:00 PM Pacific Time, both in-person and online. <ref>https://shintoinari.org/2024saitan-202401tsukinami/</ref>
For the most current information on upcoming Tsukinami-sai ceremonies and other events, it's advisable to visit the Shinto Shrine of Shusse Inari in America's official website or their YouTube channel. <ref>https://www.youtube.com/%40shusseinarishrineofamerica1913</ref>

'''Tsukinami no Matsuri''' is one of the {{ill|Shinto|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto|qq=}} {{ill|Festival|lt=festivals|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Festival|qq=}} . At {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Shrine|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Grand Shrine|qq=}}, the three festivals held in {{ill|June|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/June|qq=}} and {{ill|December|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/December|qq=}} and the [[:en:Kannamesai Festival|Kannamesai Festival]] are called the Sansetsu Festival and Sanjisai Festival.
Tsukinami-no-Matsuri, meaning "monthly festival," is a significant Shinto ritual traditionally observed at the Ise Grand Shrine in Japan. Despite its name, the festival is held biannually, on the 11th day of June and December. It is one of the Sansetsu-sai festivals, along with the Kanname-sai festival in October, collectively known as the three major festivals of Ise Shrine. <ref name=":0">https://www.japanesewiki.com/Shinto/Tsukinami-no-Matsuri.html</ref>
Historically, during Tsukinami-no-Matsuri, the Imperial Court would present offerings, known as heihaku (silk offerings), to 304 deities worshipped in the Kinai region. This practice, referred to as hanhei, was later conducted exclusively by the Ise Grand Shrine. Although hanhei was discontinued during the Muromachi period due to conflicts like the Ōnin War, it was reinstated in the Meiji period. <ref name=":0" />
The festival involves solemn rituals and ceremonies conducted by Shinto priests, who offer prayers for peace and prosperity. These rituals are performed under the direction of the Emperor, considered a direct descendant of Amaterasu-Omikami, the sun goddess enshrined at Ise. The ceremonies include the dedication of textiles and sacred dances known as kagura. <ref>https://www.jinjahoncho.or.jp/en/shinto/index6.html</ref>
Today, many shrines across Japan observe Tsukinami-no-Matsuri by designating a specific day each month to conduct the festival, maintaining the tradition of offering prayers and performing rituals to honor the deities and seek their blessings. <ref name=":0" />
== history == It seems that in the past it was held every month, but {{ill|Engishiki|the Engishiki|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki|qq=}} stipulates that it should be held on the 11th of June and December, and it was held at {{ill|10=ja|11=朝廷_(日本)|Imperial Court in Kyoto|lt=the Imperial Court|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} and at Ise Shrine.
On the morning of the 11th day, {{ill|Department of Divinities|lt=the Shinto priests|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Department of Divinities|qq=}} at the Imperial Court distributed {{ill|10=ja|11=幣帛|hanpei|lt=offerings|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} (hanpei) to the 304 {{ill|10=ja|11=祝_(神職)|Kami|lt=Kami|lt=Deity|lt=deities|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} of {{ill|Kansai region|the Kinai region|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansai region|qq=}} <ref name="yougo">{{Cite book|title=日本史B用語集―A併記|last=全国歴史教育研究協議会|publisher={{ill|山川出版社|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/山川出版社|qq=}}|isbn=9784634013025|edition=改訂版|series=|author-link=|access-date=|和書|qq=draft|qq=draft}}</ref> . In the evening, a ceremony called "jinkonjiki" was held at the Shinkaden Hall of {{ill|10=ja|11=中和院|Chukain Temple|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}}, in which the emperor and the gods ate grains from the previous year (old grains) <ref name="yougo" /> .
After that, handicraft offerings were only practiced at Ise Shrine, and during {{ill|Muromachi period|8=en|lt=the Muromachi period,|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi period|qq=}} handicraft offerings were abolished due to events such as {{ill|Ōnin War|lt=the Onin War|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnin War|qq=}}, but were revived after {{ill|Meiji period|lt=the Meiji period|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji period|qq=}} . Currently, many shrines across the country hold monthly festivals on specific days each month. == Shinto Shrine of Shusse Inari in America == The Shinto Shrine of Shusse Inari in America, based in Los Angeles, holds monthly Tsukinami-sai (月次祭) ceremonies to honor and express gratitude to the nature spirits, or Kami-sama. These ceremonies are typically live-streamed on their official YouTube channel, "ShintoInari," allowing participants from various locations to join. <ref>https://shintoinari.org/202412-tsukinami/</ref>
For December 2024, the Tsukinami-sai was held on Sunday, December 1st, at 7 PM Pacific Time. The ceremony included expressions of respect, gratitude, and prayers for blessings from the Kami-sama, with a closing talk titled "Living with Kami-sama in Daily Life." The shrine encourages participants to follow traditional customs, such as offering Osaisen (donations), which support the continuation of these ceremonies and their goal of establishing a physical shrine in Los Angeles. <ref>https://shintoinari.org/202412-tsukinami/</ref>
Looking ahead, the shrine has scheduled the Saitan-sai (歳旦祭), a New Year's ceremony, on January 1st, 2024, at 11:00 AM Pacific Time. This event will be held at a pop-up shrine in Little Tokyo's Weller Court, Los Angeles, and will also be live-streamed for online participants. Following this, the first Tsukinami-sai of 2024 is planned for the same day at 3:00 PM Pacific Time, both in-person and online. <ref>https://shintoinari.org/2024saitan-202401tsukinami/</ref>
For the most current information on upcoming Tsukinami-sai ceremonies and other events, it's advisable to visit the Shinto Shrine of Shusse Inari in America's official website or their YouTube channel. <ref>https://www.youtube.com/%40shusseinarishrineofamerica1913</ref>
