'''Tsukinami no Matsuri''' is one of the {{ill|Shinto|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto|qq=}} {{ill|Festival|lt=festivals|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Festival|qq=}} . At {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Shrine|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Grand Shrine|qq=}}, the three festivals held in {{ill|June|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/June|qq=}} and {{ill|December|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/December|qq=}} and the [[:en:Kannamesai Festival|Kannamesai Festival]] are called the Sansetsu Festival and Sanjisai Festival.
Tsukinami-no-Matsuri, meaning "monthly festival," is a significant Shinto ritual traditionally observed at the Ise Grand Shrine in Japan. Despite its name, the festival is held biannually, on the 11th day of June and December. It is one of the Sansetsu-sai festivals, along with the Kanname-sai festival in October, collectively known as the three major festivals of Ise Shrine. <ref name=":0">https://www.japanesewiki.com/Shinto/Tsukinami-no-Matsuri.html</ref>
Historically, during Tsukinami-no-Matsuri, the Imperial Court would present offerings, known as heihaku (silk offerings), to 304 deities worshipped in the Kinai region. This practice, referred to as hanhei, was later conducted exclusively by the Ise Grand Shrine. Although hanhei was discontinued during the Muromachi period due to conflicts like the Ōnin War, it was reinstated in the Meiji period. <ref name=":0" />
The festival involves solemn rituals and ceremonies conducted by Shinto priests, who offer prayers for peace and prosperity. These rituals are performed under the direction of the Emperor, considered a direct descendant of Amaterasu-Omikami, the sun goddess enshrined at Ise. The ceremonies include the dedication of textiles and sacred dances known as kagura. <ref>https://www.jinjahoncho.or.jp/en/shinto/index6.html</ref>
Today, many shrines across Japan observe Tsukinami-no-Matsuri by designating a specific day each month to conduct the festival, maintaining the tradition of offering prayers and performing rituals to honor the deities and seek their blessings. <ref name=":0" />
== history == It seems that in the past it was held every month, but {{ill|Engishiki|the Engishiki|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki|qq=}} stipulates that it should be held on the 11th of June and December, and it was held at {{ill|10=ja|11=朝廷_(日本)|Imperial Court in Kyoto|lt=the Imperial Court|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Imperial Court of Japan|WD=Q30253177}} and at Ise Shrine.
On the morning of the 11th day, {{ill|Department of Divinities|lt=the Shinto priests|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Department of Divinities|qq=}} at the Imperial Court distributed {{ill|10=ja|11=幣帛|hanpei|lt=offerings|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} (hanpei) to the 304 {{ill|10=ja|11=祝_(神職)|Kami|lt=Kami|lt=Deity|lt=deities|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} of {{ill|Kansai region|the Kinai region|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansai region|qq=}} <ref name="yougo">{{Cite book|title=日本史B用語集―A併記|last=全国歴史教育研究協議会|publisher={{ill|山川出版社|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/山川出版社|qq=}}|isbn=9784634013025|edition=改訂版|series=|author-link=|access-date=|和書|qq=draft|qq=draft}}</ref> . In the evening, a ceremony called "jinkonjiki" was held at the Shinkaden Hall of {{ill|10=ja|11=中和院|Chukain Temple|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}}, in which the emperor and the gods ate grains from the previous year (old grains) <ref name="yougo" /> .
After that, handicraft offerings were only practiced at Ise Shrine, and during {{ill|Muromachi period|8=en|lt=the Muromachi period,|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi period|qq=}} handicraft offerings were abolished due to events such as {{ill|Ōnin War|lt=the Onin War|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnin War|qq=}}, but were revived after {{ill|Meiji period|lt=the Meiji period|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji period|qq=}} . Currently, many shrines across the country hold monthly festivals on specific days each month. == Shinto Shrine of Shusse Inari in America == The Shinto Shrine of Shusse Inari in America, based in Los Angeles, holds monthly Tsukinami-sai (月次祭) ceremonies to honor and express gratitude to the nature spirits, or Kami-sama. These ceremonies are typically live-streamed on their official YouTube channel, "ShintoInari," allowing participants from various locations to join. <ref>https://shintoinari.org/202412-tsukinami/</ref>
For December 2024, the Tsukinami-sai was held on Sunday, December 1st, at 7 PM Pacific Time. The ceremony included expressions of respect, gratitude, and prayers for blessings from the Kami-sama, with a closing talk titled "Living with Kami-sama in Daily Life." The shrine encourages participants to follow traditional customs, such as offering Osaisen (donations), which support the continuation of these ceremonies and their goal of establishing a physical shrine in Los Angeles. <ref>https://shintoinari.org/202412-tsukinami/</ref>
Looking ahead, the shrine has scheduled the Saitan-sai (歳旦祭), a New Year's ceremony, on January 1st, 2024, at 11:00 AM Pacific Time. This event will be held at a pop-up shrine in Little Tokyo's Weller Court, Los Angeles, and will also be live-streamed for online participants. Following this, the first Tsukinami-sai of 2024 is planned for the same day at 3:00 PM Pacific Time, both in-person and online. <ref>https://shintoinari.org/2024saitan-202401tsukinami/</ref>
For the most current information on upcoming Tsukinami-sai ceremonies and other events, it's advisable to visit the Shinto Shrine of Shusse Inari in America's official website or their YouTube channel. <ref>https://www.youtube.com/%40shusseinarishrineofamerica1913</ref>

On the morning of the 11th day, {{ill|Department of Divinities|lt=the Shinto priests|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Department of Divinities|qq=}} at the Imperial Court distributed {{ill|10=ja|11=幣帛|hanpei|lt=offerings|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} (hanpei) to the 304 {{ill|10=ja|11=祝_(神職)|Kami|lt=Kami|lt=Deity|lt=deities|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} of {{ill|Kansai region|the Kinai region|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansai region|qq=}} <ref name="yougo">{{Cite book|title=日本史B用語集―A併記|last=全国歴史教育研究協議会|publisher={{ill|山川出版社|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/山川出版社|qq=}}|isbn=9784634013025|edition=改訂版|series=|author-link=|access-date=|和書|qq=draft|qq=draft}}</ref> . In the evening, a ceremony called "jinkonjiki" was held at the Shinkaden Hall of {{ill|10=ja|11=中和院|Chukain Temple|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}}, in which the emperor and the gods ate grains from the previous year (old grains) <ref name="yougo" /> .
After that, handicraft offerings were only practiced at Ise Shrine, and during {{ill|Muromachi period|8=en|lt=the Muromachi period,|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi period|qq=}} handicraft offerings were abolished due to events such as {{ill|Ōnin War|lt=the Onin War|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnin War|qq=}}, but were revived after {{ill|Meiji period|lt=the Meiji period|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji period|qq=}} . Currently, many shrines across the country hold monthly festivals on specific days each month. == Shinto Shrine of Shusse Inari in America == The Shinto Shrine of Shusse Inari in America, based in Los Angeles, holds monthly Tsukinami-sai (月次祭) ceremonies to honor and express gratitude to the nature spirits, or Kami-sama. These ceremonies are typically live-streamed on their official YouTube channel, "ShintoInari," allowing participants from various locations to join. <ref>https://shintoinari.org/202412-tsukinami/</ref>
For December 2024, the Tsukinami-sai was held on Sunday, December 1st, at 7 PM Pacific Time. The ceremony included expressions of respect, gratitude, and prayers for blessings from the Kami-sama, with a closing talk titled "Living with Kami-sama in Daily Life." The shrine encourages participants to follow traditional customs, such as offering Osaisen (donations), which support the continuation of these ceremonies and their goal of establishing a physical shrine in Los Angeles. <ref>https://shintoinari.org/202412-tsukinami/</ref>
Looking ahead, the shrine has scheduled the Saitan-sai (歳旦祭), a New Year's ceremony, on January 1st, 2024, at 11:00 AM Pacific Time. This event will be held at a pop-up shrine in Little Tokyo's Weller Court, Los Angeles, and will also be live-streamed for online participants. Following this, the first Tsukinami-sai of 2024 is planned for the same day at 3:00 PM Pacific Time, both in-person and online. <ref>https://shintoinari.org/2024saitan-202401tsukinami/</ref>
For the most current information on upcoming Tsukinami-sai ceremonies and other events, it's advisable to visit the Shinto Shrine of Shusse Inari in America's official website or their YouTube channel. <ref>https://www.youtube.com/%40shusseinarishrineofamerica1913</ref>

'''Tsukinami no Matsuri''' is one of the {{ill|Shinto|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto|qq=}} {{ill|Festival|lt=festivals|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Festival|qq=}} . At {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Shrine|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Grand Shrine|qq=}}, the three festivals held in {{ill|June|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/June|qq=}} and {{ill|December|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/December|qq=}} and the [[:en:Kannamesai Festival|Kannamesai Festival]] are called the Sansetsu Festival and Sanjisai Festival.
Tsukinami-no-Matsuri, meaning "monthly festival," is a significant Shinto ritual traditionally observed at the Ise Grand Shrine in Japan. Despite its name, the festival is held biannually, on the 11th day of June and December. It is one of the Sansetsu-sai festivals, along with the Kanname-sai festival in October, collectively known as the three major festivals of Ise Shrine. <ref name=":0">https://www.japanesewiki.com/Shinto/Tsukinami-no-Matsuri.html</ref>
Historically, during Tsukinami-no-Matsuri, the Imperial Court would present offerings, known as heihaku (silk offerings), to 304 deities worshipped in the Kinai region. This practice, referred to as hanhei, was later conducted exclusively by the Ise Grand Shrine. Although hanhei was discontinued during the Muromachi period due to conflicts like the Ōnin War, it was reinstated in the Meiji period. <ref name=":0" />
The festival involves solemn rituals and ceremonies conducted by Shinto priests, who offer prayers for peace and prosperity. These rituals are performed under the direction of the Emperor, considered a direct descendant of Amaterasu-Omikami, the sun goddess enshrined at Ise. The ceremonies include the dedication of textiles and sacred dances known as kagura. <ref>https://www.jinjahoncho.or.jp/en/shinto/index6.html</ref>
Today, many shrines across Japan observe Tsukinami-no-Matsuri by designating a specific day each month to conduct the festival, maintaining the tradition of offering prayers and performing rituals to honor the deities and seek their blessings. <ref name=":0" />
== history == It seems that in the past it was held every month, but {{ill|Engishiki|the Engishiki|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki|qq=}} stipulates that it should be held on the 11th of June and December, and it was held at {{ill|10=ja|11=朝廷_(日本)|Imperial Court in Kyoto|lt=the Imperial Court|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Imperial Court of Japan|WD=Q30253177}} and at Ise Shrine.
On the morning of the 11th day, {{ill|Department of Divinities|lt=the Shinto priests|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Department of Divinities|qq=}} at the Imperial Court distributed {{ill|10=ja|11=幣帛|hanpei|lt=offerings|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} (hanpei) to the 304 {{ill|10=ja|11=祝_(神職)|Kami|lt=Kami|lt=Deity|lt=deities|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} of {{ill|Kansai region|the Kinai region|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansai region|qq=}} <ref name="yougo">{{Cite book|title=日本史B用語集―A併記|last=全国歴史教育研究協議会|publisher={{ill|山川出版社|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/山川出版社|qq=}}|isbn=9784634013025|edition=改訂版|series=|author-link=|access-date=|和書|qq=draft|qq=draft}}</ref> . In the evening, a ceremony called "jinkonjiki" was held at the Shinkaden Hall of {{ill|10=ja|11=中和院|Chukain Temple|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}}, in which the emperor and the gods ate grains from the previous year (old grains) <ref name="yougo" /> .
After that, handicraft offerings were only practiced at Ise Shrine, and during {{ill|Muromachi period|8=en|lt=the Muromachi period,|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi period|qq=}} handicraft offerings were abolished due to events such as {{ill|Ōnin War|lt=the Onin War|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnin War|qq=}}, but were revived after {{ill|Meiji period|lt=the Meiji period|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji period|qq=}} . Currently, many shrines across the country hold monthly festivals on specific days each month. == Shinto Shrine of Shusse Inari in America == The Shinto Shrine of Shusse Inari in America, based in Los Angeles, holds monthly Tsukinami-sai (月次祭) ceremonies to honor and express gratitude to the nature spirits, or Kami-sama. These ceremonies are typically live-streamed on their official YouTube channel, "ShintoInari," allowing participants from various locations to join. <ref>https://shintoinari.org/202412-tsukinami/</ref>
For December 2024, the Tsukinami-sai was held on Sunday, December 1st, at 7 PM Pacific Time. The ceremony included expressions of respect, gratitude, and prayers for blessings from the Kami-sama, with a closing talk titled "Living with Kami-sama in Daily Life." The shrine encourages participants to follow traditional customs, such as offering Osaisen (donations), which support the continuation of these ceremonies and their goal of establishing a physical shrine in Los Angeles. <ref>https://shintoinari.org/202412-tsukinami/</ref>
Looking ahead, the shrine has scheduled the Saitan-sai (歳旦祭), a New Year's ceremony, on January 1st, 2024, at 11:00 AM Pacific Time. This event will be held at a pop-up shrine in Little Tokyo's Weller Court, Los Angeles, and will also be live-streamed for online participants. Following this, the first Tsukinami-sai of 2024 is planned for the same day at 3:00 PM Pacific Time, both in-person and online. <ref>https://shintoinari.org/2024saitan-202401tsukinami/</ref>
For the most current information on upcoming Tsukinami-sai ceremonies and other events, it's advisable to visit the Shinto Shrine of Shusse Inari in America's official website or their YouTube channel. <ref>https://www.youtube.com/%40shusseinarishrineofamerica1913</ref>

In the evening, a ceremony called "jinkonjiki" was held at the Shinkaden Hall of {{ill|10=ja|11=中和院|Chukain Temple|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}}, in which the emperor and the gods ate grains from the previous year (old grains) <ref name="yougo" /> .
After that, handicraft offerings were only practiced at Ise Shrine, and during {{ill|Muromachi period|8=en|lt=the Muromachi period,|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi period|qq=}} handicraft offerings were abolished due to events such as {{ill|Ōnin War|lt=the Onin War|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnin War|qq=}}, but were revived after {{ill|Meiji period|lt=the Meiji period|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji period|qq=}} . Currently, many shrines across the country hold monthly festivals on specific days each month. == Shinto Shrine of Shusse Inari in America == The Shinto Shrine of Shusse Inari in America, based in Los Angeles, holds monthly Tsukinami-sai (月次祭) ceremonies to honor and express gratitude to the nature spirits, or Kami-sama. These ceremonies are typically live-streamed on their official YouTube channel, "ShintoInari," allowing participants from various locations to join. <ref>https://shintoinari.org/202412-tsukinami/</ref>
For December 2024, the Tsukinami-sai was held on Sunday, December 1st, at 7 PM Pacific Time. The ceremony included expressions of respect, gratitude, and prayers for blessings from the Kami-sama, with a closing talk titled "Living with Kami-sama in Daily Life." The shrine encourages participants to follow traditional customs, such as offering Osaisen (donations), which support the continuation of these ceremonies and their goal of establishing a physical shrine in Los Angeles. <ref>https://shintoinari.org/202412-tsukinami/</ref>
Looking ahead, the shrine has scheduled the Saitan-sai (歳旦祭), a New Year's ceremony, on January 1st, 2024, at 11:00 AM Pacific Time. This event will be held at a pop-up shrine in Little Tokyo's Weller Court, Los Angeles, and will also be live-streamed for online participants. Following this, the first Tsukinami-sai of 2024 is planned for the same day at 3:00 PM Pacific Time, both in-person and online. <ref>https://shintoinari.org/2024saitan-202401tsukinami/</ref>
For the most current information on upcoming Tsukinami-sai ceremonies and other events, it's advisable to visit the Shinto Shrine of Shusse Inari in America's official website or their YouTube channel. <ref>https://www.youtube.com/%40shusseinarishrineofamerica1913</ref>

'''Tsukinami no Matsuri''' is one of the {{ill|Shinto|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto|qq=}} {{ill|Festival|lt=festivals|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Festival|qq=}} . At {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Shrine|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Grand Shrine|qq=}}, the three festivals held in {{ill|June|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/June|qq=}} and {{ill|December|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/December|qq=}} and the [[:en:Kannamesai Festival|Kannamesai Festival]] are called the Sansetsu Festival and Sanjisai Festival.
Tsukinami-no-Matsuri, meaning "monthly festival," is a significant Shinto ritual traditionally observed at the Ise Grand Shrine in Japan. Despite its name, the festival is held biannually, on the 11th day of June and December. It is one of the Sansetsu-sai festivals, along with the Kanname-sai festival in October, collectively known as the three major festivals of Ise Shrine. <ref name=":0">https://www.japanesewiki.com/Shinto/Tsukinami-no-Matsuri.html</ref>
Historically, during Tsukinami-no-Matsuri, the Imperial Court would present offerings, known as heihaku (silk offerings), to 304 deities worshipped in the Kinai region. This practice, referred to as hanhei, was later conducted exclusively by the Ise Grand Shrine. Although hanhei was discontinued during the Muromachi period due to conflicts like the Ōnin War, it was reinstated in the Meiji period. <ref name=":0" />
The festival involves solemn rituals and ceremonies conducted by Shinto priests, who offer prayers for peace and prosperity. These rituals are performed under the direction of the Emperor, considered a direct descendant of Amaterasu-Omikami, the sun goddess enshrined at Ise. The ceremonies include the dedication of textiles and sacred dances known as kagura. <ref>https://www.jinjahoncho.or.jp/en/shinto/index6.html</ref>
Today, many shrines across Japan observe Tsukinami-no-Matsuri by designating a specific day each month to conduct the festival, maintaining the tradition of offering prayers and performing rituals to honor the deities and seek their blessings. <ref name=":0" />
== history == It seems that in the past it was held every month, but {{ill|Engishiki|the Engishiki|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki|qq=}} stipulates that it should be held on the 11th of June and December, and it was held at {{ill|10=ja|11=朝廷_(日本)|Imperial Court in Kyoto|lt=the Imperial Court|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Imperial Court of Japan|WD=Q30253177}} and at Ise Shrine.
On the morning of the 11th day, {{ill|Department of Divinities|lt=the Shinto priests|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Department of Divinities|qq=}} at the Imperial Court distributed {{ill|10=ja|11=幣帛|hanpei|lt=offerings|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} (hanpei) to the 304 {{ill|10=ja|11=祝_(神職)|Kami|lt=Kami|lt=Deity|lt=deities|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} of {{ill|Kansai region|the Kinai region|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansai region|qq=}} <ref name="yougo">{{Cite book|title=日本史B用語集―A併記|last=全国歴史教育研究協議会|publisher={{ill|山川出版社|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/山川出版社|qq=}}|isbn=9784634013025|edition=改訂版|series=|author-link=|access-date=|和書|qq=draft|qq=draft}}</ref> . In the evening, a ceremony called "jinkonjiki" was held at the Shinkaden Hall of {{ill|10=ja|11=中和院|Chukain Temple|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}}, in which the emperor and the gods ate grains from the previous year (old grains) <ref name="yougo" /> .
After that, handicraft offerings were only practiced at Ise Shrine, and during {{ill|Muromachi period|8=en|lt=the Muromachi period,|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi period|qq=}} handicraft offerings were abolished due to events such as {{ill|Ōnin War|lt=the Onin War|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnin War|qq=}}, but were revived after {{ill|Meiji period|lt=the Meiji period|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji period|qq=}} . Currently, many shrines across the country hold monthly festivals on specific days each month. == Shinto Shrine of Shusse Inari in America == The Shinto Shrine of Shusse Inari in America, based in Los Angeles, holds monthly Tsukinami-sai (月次祭) ceremonies to honor and express gratitude to the nature spirits, or Kami-sama. These ceremonies are typically live-streamed on their official YouTube channel, "ShintoInari," allowing participants from various locations to join. <ref>https://shintoinari.org/202412-tsukinami/</ref>
For December 2024, the Tsukinami-sai was held on Sunday, December 1st, at 7 PM Pacific Time. The ceremony included expressions of respect, gratitude, and prayers for blessings from the Kami-sama, with a closing talk titled "Living with Kami-sama in Daily Life." The shrine encourages participants to follow traditional customs, such as offering Osaisen (donations), which support the continuation of these ceremonies and their goal of establishing a physical shrine in Los Angeles. <ref>https://shintoinari.org/202412-tsukinami/</ref>
Looking ahead, the shrine has scheduled the Saitan-sai (歳旦祭), a New Year's ceremony, on January 1st, 2024, at 11:00 AM Pacific Time. This event will be held at a pop-up shrine in Little Tokyo's Weller Court, Los Angeles, and will also be live-streamed for online participants. Following this, the first Tsukinami-sai of 2024 is planned for the same day at 3:00 PM Pacific Time, both in-person and online. <ref>https://shintoinari.org/2024saitan-202401tsukinami/</ref>
For the most current information on upcoming Tsukinami-sai ceremonies and other events, it's advisable to visit the Shinto Shrine of Shusse Inari in America's official website or their YouTube channel. <ref>https://www.youtube.com/%40shusseinarishrineofamerica1913</ref>

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q11516161]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:07, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created in the draft namespace on english wikipedia. It was then deleted but preserved on everybodywiki. The imported history from everybodywiki is patchwork and contains all the contributing users, but not all the revisions are actually present. Lets hope this is sufficient attribution though especially since most of them are mine.[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:28, 2 May 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=see for the history[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:26, 31 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=page was originally created in the draft namespace on english wikipedia. It was then copied to userspace on simple english wikipedia and edited before being imported here. The entire simple english wikipedia history is present, but the wikipedia draft can be found on everybodywiki here and should be undeleted here on wikipedia [[:en:draft:Tsukinami-no-Matsuri]] and have the revisions imported to this wiki to give proper attribution. While most edits are by Immanuelle there might be a few from other users. It was then copied to userspace on simple english wikipedia and edited before being imported here. The entire simple english wikipedia history is present, but the wikipedia draft can be found on everybodywiki here and should be undeleted here on wikipedia [[:en:draft:Tsukinami-no-Matsuri]] and have the revisions imported to this wiki to give proper attribution. {{User:Immanuelle/Ritual ceremonies of the Imperial Palace updated}}
'''Tsukinami no Matsuri''' is one of the {{ill|Shinto|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto|qq=}} {{ill|Festival|lt=festivals|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Festival|qq=}} . At {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Shrine|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Grand Shrine|qq=}}, the three festivals held in {{ill|June|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/June|qq=}} and {{ill|December|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/December|qq=}} and the [[:en:Kannamesai Festival|Kannamesai Festival]] are called the Sansetsu Festival and Sanjisai Festival.
Tsukinami-no-Matsuri, meaning "monthly festival," is a significant Shinto ritual traditionally observed at the Ise Grand Shrine in Japan. Despite its name, the festival is held biannually, on the 11th day of June and December. It is one of the Sansetsu-sai festivals, along with the Kanname-sai festival in October, collectively known as the three major festivals of Ise Shrine. <ref name=":0">https://www.japanesewiki.com/Shinto/Tsukinami-no-Matsuri.html</ref>
Historically, during Tsukinami-no-Matsuri, the Imperial Court would present offerings, known as heihaku (silk offerings), to 304 deities worshipped in the Kinai region. This practice, referred to as hanhei, was later conducted exclusively by the Ise Grand Shrine. Although hanhei was discontinued during the Muromachi period due to conflicts like the Ōnin War, it was reinstated in the Meiji period. <ref name=":0" />
The festival involves solemn rituals and ceremonies conducted by Shinto priests, who offer prayers for peace and prosperity. These rituals are performed under the direction of the Emperor, considered a direct descendant of Amaterasu-Omikami, the sun goddess enshrined at Ise. The ceremonies include the dedication of textiles and sacred dances known as kagura. <ref>https://www.jinjahoncho.or.jp/en/shinto/index6.html</ref>
Today, many shrines across Japan observe Tsukinami-no-Matsuri by designating a specific day each month to conduct the festival, maintaining the tradition of offering prayers and performing rituals to honor the deities and seek their blessings. <ref name=":0" />
== history == It seems that in the past it was held every month, but {{ill|Engishiki|the Engishiki|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki|qq=}} stipulates that it should be held on the 11th of June and December, and it was held at {{ill|10=ja|11=朝廷_(日本)|Imperial Court in Kyoto|lt=the Imperial Court|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Imperial Court of Japan|WD=Q30253177}} and at Ise Shrine.
On the morning of the 11th day, {{ill|Department of Divinities|lt=the Shinto priests|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Department of Divinities|qq=}} at the Imperial Court distributed {{ill|10=ja|11=幣帛|hanpei|lt=offerings|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} (hanpei) to the 304 {{ill|10=ja|11=祝_(神職)|Kami|lt=Kami|lt=Deity|lt=deities|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} of {{ill|Kansai region|the Kinai region|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansai region|qq=}} <ref name="yougo">{{Cite book|title=日本史B用語集―A併記|last=全国歴史教育研究協議会|publisher={{ill|山川出版社|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/山川出版社|qq=}}|isbn=9784634013025|edition=改訂版|series=|author-link=|access-date=|和書|qq=draft|qq=draft}}</ref> . In the evening, a ceremony called "jinkonjiki" was held at the Shinkaden Hall of {{ill|10=ja|11=中和院|Chukain Temple|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}}, in which the emperor and the gods ate grains from the previous year (old grains) <ref name="yougo" /> .
After that, handicraft offerings were only practiced at Ise Shrine, and during {{ill|Muromachi period|8=en|lt=the Muromachi period,|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi period|qq=}} handicraft offerings were abolished due to events such as {{ill|Ōnin War|lt=the Onin War|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnin War|qq=}}, but were revived after {{ill|Meiji period|lt=the Meiji period|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji period|qq=}} . Currently, many shrines across the country hold monthly festivals on specific days each month. == Shinto Shrine of Shusse Inari in America == The Shinto Shrine of Shusse Inari in America, based in Los Angeles, holds monthly Tsukinami-sai (月次祭) ceremonies to honor and express gratitude to the nature spirits, or Kami-sama. These ceremonies are typically live-streamed on their official YouTube channel, "ShintoInari," allowing participants from various locations to join. <ref>https://shintoinari.org/202412-tsukinami/</ref>
For December 2024, the Tsukinami-sai was held on Sunday, December 1st, at 7 PM Pacific Time. The ceremony included expressions of respect, gratitude, and prayers for blessings from the Kami-sama, with a closing talk titled "Living with Kami-sama in Daily Life." The shrine encourages participants to follow traditional customs, such as offering Osaisen (donations), which support the continuation of these ceremonies and their goal of establishing a physical shrine in Los Angeles. <ref>https://shintoinari.org/202412-tsukinami/</ref>
Looking ahead, the shrine has scheduled the Saitan-sai (歳旦祭), a New Year's ceremony, on January 1st, 2024, at 11:00 AM Pacific Time. This event will be held at a pop-up shrine in Little Tokyo's Weller Court, Los Angeles, and will also be live-streamed for online participants. Following this, the first Tsukinami-sai of 2024 is planned for the same day at 3:00 PM Pacific Time, both in-person and online. <ref>https://shintoinari.org/2024saitan-202401tsukinami/</ref>
For the most current information on upcoming Tsukinami-sai ceremonies and other events, it's advisable to visit the Shinto Shrine of Shusse Inari in America's official website or their YouTube channel. <ref>https://www.youtube.com/%40shusseinarishrineofamerica1913</ref>

{{User:Immanuelle/Ritual ceremonies of the Imperial Palace updated}}

'''Tsukinami no Matsuri''' is one of the {{ill|Shinto|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto|qq=}} {{ill|Festival|lt=festivals|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Festival|qq=}} . At {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Shrine|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Grand Shrine|qq=}}, the three festivals held in {{ill|June|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/June|qq=}} and {{ill|December|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/December|qq=}} and the [[:en:Kannamesai Festival|Kannamesai Festival]] are called the Sansetsu Festival and Sanjisai Festival.

Tsukinami-no-Matsuri, meaning "monthly festival," is a significant Shinto ritual traditionally observed at the Ise Grand Shrine in Japan. Despite its name, the festival is held biannually, on the 11th day of June and December. It is one of the Sansetsu-sai festivals, along with the Kanname-sai festival in October, collectively known as the three major festivals of Ise Shrine. <ref name=":0">https://www.japanesewiki.com/Shinto/Tsukinami-no-Matsuri.html</ref>

Historically, during Tsukinami-no-Matsuri, the Imperial Court would present offerings, known as heihaku (silk offerings), to 304 deities worshipped in the Kinai region. This practice, referred to as hanhei, was later conducted exclusively by the Ise Grand Shrine. Although hanhei was discontinued during the Muromachi period due to conflicts like the Ōnin War, it was reinstated in the Meiji period. <ref name=":0" />

The festival involves solemn rituals and ceremonies conducted by Shinto priests, who offer prayers for peace and prosperity. These rituals are performed under the direction of the Emperor, considered a direct descendant of Amaterasu-Omikami, the sun goddess enshrined at Ise. The ceremonies include the dedication of textiles and sacred dances known as kagura. <ref>https://www.jinjahoncho.or.jp/en/shinto/index6.html</ref>

Today, many shrines across Japan observe Tsukinami-no-Matsuri by designating a specific day each month to conduct the festival, maintaining the tradition of offering prayers and performing rituals to honor the deities and seek their blessings. <ref name=":0" />

== history ==
It seems that in the past it was held every month, but {{ill|Engishiki|the Engishiki|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki|qq=}} stipulates that it should be held on the 11th of June and December, and it was held at {{ill|10=ja|11=朝廷_(日本)|Imperial Court in Kyoto|lt=the Imperial Court|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Imperial Court of Japan|WD=Q30253177}} and at Ise Shrine.

On the morning of the 11th day, {{ill|Department of Divinities|lt=the Shinto priests|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Department of Divinities|qq=}} at the Imperial Court distributed {{ill|10=ja|11=幣帛|hanpei|lt=offerings|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} (hanpei) to the 304 {{ill|10=ja|11=祝_(神職)|Kami|lt=Kami|lt=Deity|lt=deities|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} of {{ill|Kansai region|the Kinai region|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansai region|qq=}} <ref name="yougo">{{Cite book|title=日本史B用語集―A併記|last=全国歴史教育研究協議会|publisher={{ill|山川出版社|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/山川出版社|qq=}}|isbn=9784634013025|edition=改訂版|series=|author-link=|access-date=|和書|qq=draft|qq=draft}}</ref> . In the evening, a ceremony called "jinkonjiki" was held at the Shinkaden Hall of {{ill|10=ja|11=中和院|Chukain Temple|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}}, in which the emperor and the gods ate grains from the previous year (old grains) <ref name="yougo" /> .

After that, handicraft offerings were only practiced at Ise Shrine, and during {{ill|Muromachi period|8=en|lt=the Muromachi period,|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi period|qq=}} handicraft offerings were abolished due to events such as {{ill|Ōnin War|lt=the Onin War|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnin War|qq=}}, but were revived after {{ill|Meiji period|lt=the Meiji period|8=en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji period|qq=}} . Currently, many shrines across the country hold monthly festivals on specific days each month.
== Shinto Shrine of Shusse Inari in America ==
The Shinto Shrine of Shusse Inari in America, based in Los Angeles, holds monthly Tsukinami-sai (月次祭) ceremonies to honor and express gratitude to the nature spirits, or Kami-sama. These ceremonies are typically live-streamed on their official YouTube channel, "ShintoInari," allowing participants from various locations to join. <ref>https://shintoinari.org/202412-tsukinami/</ref>

For December 2024, the Tsukinami-sai was held on Sunday, December 1st, at 7 PM Pacific Time. The ceremony included expressions of respect, gratitude, and prayers for blessings from the Kami-sama, with a closing talk titled "Living with Kami-sama in Daily Life." The shrine encourages participants to follow traditional customs, such as offering Osaisen (donations), which support the continuation of these ceremonies and their goal of establishing a physical shrine in Los Angeles. <ref>https://shintoinari.org/202412-tsukinami/</ref>

Looking ahead, the shrine has scheduled the Saitan-sai (歳旦祭), a New Year's ceremony, on January 1st, 2024, at 11:00 AM Pacific Time. This event will be held at a pop-up shrine in Little Tokyo's Weller Court, Los Angeles, and will also be live-streamed for online participants. Following this, the first Tsukinami-sai of 2024 is planned for the same day at 3:00 PM Pacific Time, both in-person and online. <ref>https://shintoinari.org/2024saitan-202401tsukinami/</ref>

For the most current information on upcoming Tsukinami-sai ceremonies and other events, it's advisable to visit the Shinto Shrine of Shusse Inari in America's official website or their YouTube channel. <ref>https://www.youtube.com/%40shusseinarishrineofamerica1913</ref>

'''Tsukinami no Matsuri''' is one of the {{ill|Shinto|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto|qq=}} {{ill|Festival|lt=festivals|8=en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Festival|qq=}} . At {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Shrine|8=en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Grand Shrine|qq=}}, the three festivals held in {{ill|June|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/June|qq=}} and {{ill|December|8=en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/December|qq=}} and the [[:en:Kannamesai Festival|Kannamesai Festival]] are called the Sansetsu Festival and Sanjisai Festival.

Tsukinami-no-Matsuri, meaning "monthly festival," is a significant Shinto ritual traditionally observed at the Ise Grand Shrine in Japan. Despite its name, the festival is held biannually, on the 11th day of June and December. It is one of the Sansetsu-sai festivals, along with the Kanname-sai festival in October, collectively known as the three major festivals of Ise Shrine. <ref name=":0">https://www.japanesewiki.com/Shinto/Tsukinami-no-Matsuri.html</ref>

Historically, during Tsukinami-no-Matsuri, the Imperial Court would present offerings, known as heihaku (silk offerings), to 304 deities worshipped in the Kinai region. This practice, referred to as hanhei, was later conducted exclusively by the Ise Grand Shrine. Although hanhei was discontinued during the Muromachi period due to conflicts like the Ōnin War, it was reinstated in the Meiji period. <ref name=":0" />

The festival involves solemn rituals and ceremonies conducted by Shinto priests, who offer prayers for peace and prosperity. These rituals are performed under the direction of the Emperor, considered a direct descendant of Amaterasu-Omikami, the sun goddess enshrined at Ise. The ceremonies include the dedication of textiles and sacred dances known as kagura. <ref>https://www.jinjahoncho.or.jp/en/shinto/index6.html</ref>

Today, many shrines across Japan observe Tsukinami-no-Matsuri by designating a specific day each month to conduct the festival, maintaining the tradition of offering prayers and performing rituals to honor the deities and seek their blessings. <ref name=":0" />

== history ==
It seems that in the past it was held every month, but {{ill|Engishiki|the Engishiki|8=en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki|qq=}} stipulates that it should be held on the 11th of June and December, and it was held at {{ill|10=ja|11=朝廷_(日本)|Imperial Court in Kyoto|lt=the Imperial Court|8=en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Imperial Court of Japan|WD=Q30253177}} and at Ise Shrine.

On the morning of the 11th day, {{ill|Department of Divinities|lt=the Shinto priests|8=en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Department of Divinities|qq=}} at the Imperial Court distributed {{ill|10=ja|11=幣帛|hanpei|lt=offerings|8=en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} (hanpei) to the 304 {{ill|10=ja|11=祝_(神職)|Kami|lt=Kami|lt=Deity|lt=deities|8=en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} of {{ill|Kansai region|the Kinai region|8=en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansai region|qq=}} <ref name="yougo">{{Cite book|title=日本史B用語集―A併記|last=全国歴史教育研究協議会|publisher={{ill|山川出版社|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/山川出版社|qq=}}|isbn=9784634013025|edition=改訂版|series=|author-link=|access-date=|和書|qq=draft}}</ref> . In the evening, a ceremony called "jinkonjiki" was held at the Shinkaden Hall of {{ill|10=ja|11=中和院|Chukain Temple|8=en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}}, in which the emperor and the gods ate grains from the previous year (old grains) <ref name="yougo" /> .

After that, handicraft offerings were only practiced at Ise Shrine, and during {{ill|Muromachi period|8=en|lt=the Muromachi period,|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi period|qq=}} handicraft offerings were abolished due to events such as {{ill|Ōnin War|lt=the Onin War|8=en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnin War|qq=}}, but were revived after {{ill|Meiji period|lt=the Meiji period|8=en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji period|qq=}} . Currently, many shrines across the country hold monthly festivals on specific days each month.
== Shinto Shrine of Shusse Inari in America ==
The Shinto Shrine of Shusse Inari in America, based in Los Angeles, holds monthly Tsukinami-sai (月次祭) ceremonies to honor and express gratitude to the nature spirits, or Kami-sama. These ceremonies are typically live-streamed on their official YouTube channel, "ShintoInari," allowing participants from various locations to join. <ref>https://shintoinari.org/202412-tsukinami/</ref>

For December 2024, the Tsukinami-sai was held on Sunday, December 1st, at 7 PM Pacific Time. The ceremony included expressions of respect, gratitude, and prayers for blessings from the Kami-sama, with a closing talk titled "Living with Kami-sama in Daily Life." The shrine encourages participants to follow traditional customs, such as offering Osaisen (donations), which support the continuation of these ceremonies and their goal of establishing a physical shrine in Los Angeles. <ref>https://shintoinari.org/202412-tsukinami/</ref>

Looking ahead, the shrine has scheduled the Saitan-sai (歳旦祭), a New Year's ceremony, on January 1st, 2024, at 11:00 AM Pacific Time. This event will be held at a pop-up shrine in Little Tokyo's Weller Court, Los Angeles, and will also be live-streamed for online participants. Following this, the first Tsukinami-sai of 2024 is planned for the same day at 3:00 PM Pacific Time, both in-person and online. <ref>https://shintoinari.org/2024saitan-202401tsukinami/</ref>

For the most current information on upcoming Tsukinami-sai ceremonies and other events, it's advisable to visit the Shinto Shrine of Shusse Inari in America's official website or their YouTube channel. <ref>https://www.youtube.com/%40shusseinarishrineofamerica1913</ref>

In the evening, a ceremony called "jinkonjiki" was held at the Shinkaden Hall of {{ill|10=ja|11=中和院|Chukain Temple|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}}, in which the emperor and the gods ate grains from the previous year (old grains) <ref name="yougo" /> .

After that, handicraft offerings were only practiced at Ise Shrine, and during {{ill|Muromachi period|8=en|lt=the Muromachi period,|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi period|qq=}} handicraft offerings were abolished due to events such as {{ill|Ōnin War|lt=the Onin War|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnin War|qq=}}, but were revived after {{ill|Meiji period|lt=the Meiji period|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji period|qq=}} . Currently, many shrines across the country hold monthly festivals on specific days each month.
== Shinto Shrine of Shusse Inari in America ==
The Shinto Shrine of Shusse Inari in America, based in Los Angeles, holds monthly Tsukinami-sai (月次祭) ceremonies to honor and express gratitude to the nature spirits, or Kami-sama. These ceremonies are typically live-streamed on their official YouTube channel, "ShintoInari," allowing participants from various locations to join. <ref>https://shintoinari.org/202412-tsukinami/</ref>

For December 2024, the Tsukinami-sai was held on Sunday, December 1st, at 7 PM Pacific Time. The ceremony included expressions of respect, gratitude, and prayers for blessings from the Kami-sama, with a closing talk titled "Living with Kami-sama in Daily Life." The shrine encourages participants to follow traditional customs, such as offering Osaisen (donations), which support the continuation of these ceremonies and their goal of establishing a physical shrine in Los Angeles. <ref>https://shintoinari.org/202412-tsukinami/</ref>

Looking ahead, the shrine has scheduled the Saitan-sai (歳旦祭), a New Year's ceremony, on January 1st, 2024, at 11:00 AM Pacific Time. This event will be held at a pop-up shrine in Little Tokyo's Weller Court, Los Angeles, and will also be live-streamed for online participants. Following this, the first Tsukinami-sai of 2024 is planned for the same day at 3:00 PM Pacific Time, both in-person and online. <ref>https://shintoinari.org/2024saitan-202401tsukinami/</ref>

For the most current information on upcoming Tsukinami-sai ceremonies and other events, it's advisable to visit the Shinto Shrine of Shusse Inari in America's official website or their YouTube channel. <ref>https://www.youtube.com/%40shusseinarishrineofamerica1913</ref>

'''Tsukinami no Matsuri''' is one of the {{ill|Shinto|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto|qq=}} {{ill|Festival|lt=festivals|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Festival|qq=}} . At {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Shrine|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Grand Shrine|qq=}}, the three festivals held in {{ill|June|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/June|qq=}} and {{ill|December|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/December|qq=}} and the [[:en:Kannamesai Festival|Kannamesai Festival]] are called the Sansetsu Festival and Sanjisai Festival.

Tsukinami-no-Matsuri, meaning "monthly festival," is a significant Shinto ritual traditionally observed at the Ise Grand Shrine in Japan. Despite its name, the festival is held biannually, on the 11th day of June and December. It is one of the Sansetsu-sai festivals, along with the Kanname-sai festival in October, collectively known as the three major festivals of Ise Shrine. <ref name=":0">https://www.japanesewiki.com/Shinto/Tsukinami-no-Matsuri.html</ref>

Historically, during Tsukinami-no-Matsuri, the Imperial Court would present offerings, known as heihaku (silk offerings), to 304 deities worshipped in the Kinai region. This practice, referred to as hanhei, was later conducted exclusively by the Ise Grand Shrine. Although hanhei was discontinued during the Muromachi period due to conflicts like the Ōnin War, it was reinstated in the Meiji period. <ref name=":0" />

The festival involves solemn rituals and ceremonies conducted by Shinto priests, who offer prayers for peace and prosperity. These rituals are performed under the direction of the Emperor, considered a direct descendant of Amaterasu-Omikami, the sun goddess enshrined at Ise. The ceremonies include the dedication of textiles and sacred dances known as kagura. <ref>https://www.jinjahoncho.or.jp/en/shinto/index6.html</ref>

Today, many shrines across Japan observe Tsukinami-no-Matsuri by designating a specific day each month to conduct the festival, maintaining the tradition of offering prayers and performing rituals to honor the deities and seek their blessings. <ref name=":0" />

== history ==
It seems that in the past it was held every month, but {{ill|Engishiki|the Engishiki|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki|qq=}} stipulates that it should be held on the 11th of June and December, and it was held at {{ill|10=ja|11=朝廷_(日本)|Imperial Court in Kyoto|lt=the Imperial Court|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Imperial Court of Japan|WD=Q30253177}} and at Ise Shrine.

On the morning of the 11th day, {{ill|Department of Divinities|lt=the Shinto priests|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Department of Divinities|qq=}} at the Imperial Court distributed {{ill|10=ja|11=幣帛|hanpei|lt=offerings|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} (hanpei) to the 304 {{ill|10=ja|11=祝_(神職)|Kami|lt=Kami|lt=Deity|lt=deities|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} of {{ill|Kansai region|the Kinai region|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansai region|qq=}} <ref name="yougo">{{Cite book|title=日本史B用語集―A併記|last=全国歴史教育研究協議会|publisher={{ill|山川出版社|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/山川出版社|qq=}}|isbn=9784634013025|edition=改訂版|series=|author-link=|access-date=|和書|qq=draft}}</ref> . In the evening, a ceremony called "jinkonjiki" was held at the Shinkaden Hall of {{ill|10=ja|11=中和院|Chukain Temple|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}}, in which the emperor and the gods ate grains from the previous year (old grains) <ref name="yougo" /> .

After that, handicraft offerings were only practiced at Ise Shrine, and during {{ill|Muromachi period|8=en|lt=the Muromachi period,|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi period|qq=}} handicraft offerings were abolished due to events such as {{ill|Ōnin War|lt=the Onin War|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnin War|qq=}}, but were revived after {{ill|Meiji period|lt=the Meiji period|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji period|qq=}} . Currently, many shrines across the country hold monthly festivals on specific days each month.

For December 2024, the Tsukinami-sai was held on Sunday, December 1st, at 7 PM Pacific Time. The ceremony included expressions of respect, gratitude, and prayers for blessings from the Kami-sama, with a closing talk titled "Living with Kami-sama in Daily Life." The shrine encourages participants to follow traditional customs, such as offering Osaisen (donations), which support the continuation of these ceremonies and their goal of establishing a physical shrine in Los Angeles. <ref>https://shintoinari.org/202412-tsukinami/</ref>

Looking ahead, the shrine has scheduled the Saitan-sai (歳旦祭), a New Year's ceremony, on January 1st, 2024, at 11:00 AM Pacific Time. This event will be held at a pop-up shrine in Little Tokyo's Weller Court, Los Angeles, and will also be live-streamed for online participants. Following this, the first Tsukinami-sai of 2024 is planned for the same day at 3:00 PM Pacific Time, both in-person and online. <ref>https://shintoinari.org/2024saitan-202401tsukinami/</ref>

For the most current information on upcoming Tsukinami-sai ceremonies and other events, it's advisable to visit the Shinto Shrine of Shusse Inari in America's official website or their YouTube channel. <ref>https://www.youtube.com/%40shusseinarishrineofamerica1913</ref>

'''Tsukinami no Matsuri''' is one of the {{ill|Shinto|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto|qq=}} {{ill|Festival|lt=festivals|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Festival|qq=}} . At {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Shrine|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Grand Shrine|qq=}}, the three festivals held in {{ill|June|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/June|qq=}} and {{ill|December|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/December|qq=}} and the [[:en:Kannamesai Festival|Kannamesai Festival]] are called the Sansetsu Festival and Sanjisai Festival.

Tsukinami-no-Matsuri, meaning "monthly festival," is a significant Shinto ritual traditionally observed at the Ise Grand Shrine in Japan. Despite its name, the festival is held biannually, on the 11th day of June and December. It is one of the Sansetsu-sai festivals, along with the Kanname-sai festival in October, collectively known as the three major festivals of Ise Shrine. <ref name=":0">https://www.japanesewiki.com/Shinto/Tsukinami-no-Matsuri.html</ref>

Historically, during Tsukinami-no-Matsuri, the Imperial Court would present offerings, known as heihaku (silk offerings), to 304 deities worshipped in the Kinai region. This practice, referred to as hanhei, was later conducted exclusively by the Ise Grand Shrine. Although hanhei was discontinued during the Muromachi period due to conflicts like the Ōnin War, it was reinstated in the Meiji period. <ref name=":0" />

The festival involves solemn rituals and ceremonies conducted by Shinto priests, who offer prayers for peace and prosperity. These rituals are performed under the direction of the Emperor, considered a direct descendant of Amaterasu-Omikami, the sun goddess enshrined at Ise. The ceremonies include the dedication of textiles and sacred dances known as kagura. <ref>https://www.jinjahoncho.or.jp/en/shinto/index6.html</ref>

Today, many shrines across Japan observe Tsukinami-no-Matsuri by designating a specific day each month to conduct the festival, maintaining the tradition of offering prayers and performing rituals to honor the deities and seek their blessings. <ref name=":0" />

== history ==
It seems that in the past it was held every month, but {{ill|Engishiki|the Engishiki|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki|qq=}} stipulates that it should be held on the 11th of June and December, and it was held at {{ill|10=ja|11=朝廷_(日本)|Imperial Court in Kyoto|lt=the Imperial Court|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Imperial Court of Japan|WD=Q30253177}} and at Ise Shrine.

On the morning of the 11th day, {{ill|Department of Divinities|lt=the Shinto priests|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Department of Divinities|qq=}} at the Imperial Court distributed {{ill|10=ja|11=幣帛|hanpei|lt=offerings|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} (hanpei) to the 304 {{ill|10=ja|11=祝_(神職)|Kami|lt=Kami|lt=Deity|lt=deities|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} of {{ill|Kansai region|the Kinai region|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansai region|qq=}} <ref name="yougo">{{Cite book|title=日本史B用語集―A併記|last=全国歴史教育研究協議会|publisher={{ill|山川出版社|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/山川出版社|qq=}}|isbn=9784634013025|edition=改訂版|series=|author-link=|access-date=|和書|qq=draft}}</ref> . In the evening, a ceremony called "jinkonjiki" was held at the Shinkaden Hall of {{ill|10=ja|11=中和院|Chukain Temple|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}}, in which the emperor and the gods ate grains from the previous year (old grains) <ref name="yougo" /> .

After that, handicraft offerings were only practiced at Ise Shrine, and during {{ill|Muromachi period|8=en|lt=the Muromachi period,|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi period|qq=}} handicraft offerings were abolished due to events such as {{ill|Ōnin War|lt=the Onin War|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnin War|qq=}}, but were revived after {{ill|Meiji period|lt=the Meiji period|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji period|qq=}} . Currently, many shrines across the country hold monthly festivals on specific days each month.
== Shinto Shrine of Shusse Inari in America ==
The Shinto Shrine of Shusse Inari in America, based in Los Angeles, holds monthly Tsukinami-sai (月次祭) ceremonies to honor and express gratitude to the nature spirits, or Kami-sama. These ceremonies are typically live-streamed on their official YouTube channel, "ShintoInari," allowing participants from various locations to join. <ref>https://shintoinari.org/202412-tsukinami/</ref>

For December 2024, the Tsukinami-sai was held on Sunday, December 1st, at 7 PM Pacific Time. The ceremony included expressions of respect, gratitude, and prayers for blessings from the Kami-sama, with a closing talk titled "Living with Kami-sama in Daily Life." The shrine encourages participants to follow traditional customs, such as offering Osaisen (donations), which support the continuation of these ceremonies and their goal of establishing a physical shrine in Los Angeles. <ref>https://shintoinari.org/202412-tsukinami/</ref>

Looking ahead, the shrine has scheduled the Saitan-sai (歳旦祭), a New Year's ceremony, on January 1st, 2024, at 11:00 AM Pacific Time. This event will be held at a pop-up shrine in Little Tokyo's Weller Court, Los Angeles, and will also be live-streamed for online participants. Following this, the first Tsukinami-sai of 2024 is planned for the same day at 3:00 PM Pacific Time, both in-person and online. <ref>https://shintoinari.org/2024saitan-202401tsukinami/</ref>

For the most current information on upcoming Tsukinami-sai ceremonies and other events, it's advisable to visit the Shinto Shrine of Shusse Inari in America's official website or their YouTube channel. <ref>https://www.youtube.com/%40shusseinarishrineofamerica1913</ref>

{{User:Immanuelle/Ritual ceremonies of the Imperial Palace updated}}

'''Tsukinami no Matsuri''' is one of the {{ill|Shinto|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto|qq=}} {{ill|Festival|lt=festivals|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Festival|qq=}} . At {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Shrine|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Grand Shrine|qq=}}, the three festivals held in {{ill|June|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/June|qq=}} and {{ill|December|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/December|qq=}} and the [[:en:Kannamesai Festival|Kannamesai Festival]] are called the Sansetsu Festival and Sanjisai Festival.

Tsukinami-no-Matsuri, meaning "monthly festival," is a significant Shinto ritual traditionally observed at the Ise Grand Shrine in Japan. Despite its name, the festival is held biannually, on the 11th day of June and December. It is one of the Sansetsu-sai festivals, along with the Kanname-sai festival in October, collectively known as the three major festivals of Ise Shrine. <ref name=":0">https://www.japanesewiki.com/Shinto/Tsukinami-no-Matsuri.html</ref>

Historically, during Tsukinami-no-Matsuri, the Imperial Court would present offerings, known as heihaku (silk offerings), to 304 deities worshipped in the Kinai region. This practice, referred to as hanhei, was later conducted exclusively by the Ise Grand Shrine. Although hanhei was discontinued during the Muromachi period due to conflicts like the Ōnin War, it was reinstated in the Meiji period. <ref name=":0" />

The festival involves solemn rituals and ceremonies conducted by Shinto priests, who offer prayers for peace and prosperity. These rituals are performed under the direction of the Emperor, considered a direct descendant of Amaterasu-Omikami, the sun goddess enshrined at Ise. The ceremonies include the dedication of textiles and sacred dances known as kagura. <ref>https://www.jinjahoncho.or.jp/en/shinto/index6.html</ref>

Today, many shrines across Japan observe Tsukinami-no-Matsuri by designating a specific day each month to conduct the festival, maintaining the tradition of offering prayers and performing rituals to honor the deities and seek their blessings. <ref name=":0" />

== history ==
It seems that in the past it was held every month, but {{ill|Engishiki|the Engishiki|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki|qq=}} stipulates that it should be held on the 11th of June and December, and it was held at {{ill|10=ja|11=朝廷_(日本)|Imperial Court in Kyoto|lt=the Imperial Court|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Imperial Court of Japan|WD=Q30253177}} and at Ise Shrine.

On the morning of the 11th day, {{ill|Department of Divinities|lt=the Shinto priests|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Department of Divinities|qq=}} at the Imperial Court distributed {{ill|10=ja|11=幣帛|hanpei|lt=offerings|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} (hanpei) to the 304 {{ill|10=ja|11=祝_(神職)|Kami|lt=Kami|lt=Deity|lt=deities|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} of {{ill|Kansai region|the Kinai region|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansai region|qq=}} <ref name="yougo">{{Cite book|title=日本史B用語集―A併記|last=全国歴史教育研究協議会|publisher={{ill|山川出版社|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/山川出版社|qq=}}|isbn=9784634013025|edition=改訂版|series=|author-link=|access-date=|和書|qq=draft}}</ref> . In the evening, a ceremony called "jinkonjiki" was held at the Shinkaden Hall of {{ill|10=ja|11=中和院|Chukain Temple|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}}, in which the emperor and the gods ate grains from the previous year (old grains) <ref name="yougo" /> .

After that, handicraft offerings were only practiced at Ise Shrine, and during {{ill|Muromachi period|8=en|lt=the Muromachi period,|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi period|qq=}} handicraft offerings were abolished due to events such as {{ill|Ōnin War|lt=the Onin War|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnin War|qq=}}, but were revived after {{ill|Meiji period|lt=the Meiji period|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji period|qq=}} . Currently, many shrines across the country hold monthly festivals on specific days each month.
== Shinto Shrine of Shusse Inari in America ==
The Shinto Shrine of Shusse Inari in America, based in Los Angeles, holds monthly Tsukinami-sai (月次祭) ceremonies to honor and express gratitude to the nature spirits, or Kami-sama. These ceremonies are typically live-streamed on their official YouTube channel, "ShintoInari," allowing participants from various locations to join. <ref>https://shintoinari.org/202412-tsukinami/</ref>

For December 2024, the Tsukinami-sai was held on Sunday, December 1st, at 7 PM Pacific Time. The ceremony included expressions of respect, gratitude, and prayers for blessings from the Kami-sama, with a closing talk titled "Living with Kami-sama in Daily Life." The shrine encourages participants to follow traditional customs, such as offering Osaisen (donations), which support the continuation of these ceremonies and their goal of establishing a physical shrine in Los Angeles. <ref>https://shintoinari.org/202412-tsukinami/</ref>

Looking ahead, the shrine has scheduled the Saitan-sai (歳旦祭), a New Year's ceremony, on January 1st, 2024, at 11:00 AM Pacific Time. This event will be held at a pop-up shrine in Little Tokyo's Weller Court, Los Angeles, and will also be live-streamed for online participants. Following this, the first Tsukinami-sai of 2024 is planned for the same day at 3:00 PM Pacific Time, both in-person and online. <ref>https://shintoinari.org/2024saitan-202401tsukinami/</ref>

For the most current information on upcoming Tsukinami-sai ceremonies and other events, it's advisable to visit the Shinto Shrine of Shusse Inari in America's official website or their YouTube channel. <ref>https://www.youtube.com/%40shusseinarishrineofamerica1913</ref>

Despite its name, the festival is held biannually, on the 11th day of June and December. It is one of the Sansetsu-sai festivals, along with the Kanname-sai festival in October, collectively known as the three major festivals of Ise Shrine. <ref name=":0">https://www.japanesewiki.com/Shinto/Tsukinami-no-Matsuri.html</ref>

Historically, during Tsukinami-no-Matsuri, the Imperial Court would present offerings, known as heihaku (silk offerings), to 304 deities worshipped in the Kinai region. This practice, referred to as hanhei, was later conducted exclusively by the Ise Grand Shrine. Although hanhei was discontinued during the Muromachi period due to conflicts like the Ōnin War, it was reinstated in the Meiji period. <ref name=":0" />

The festival involves solemn rituals and ceremonies conducted by Shinto priests, who offer prayers for peace and prosperity. These rituals are performed under the direction of the Emperor, considered a direct descendant of Amaterasu-Omikami, the sun goddess enshrined at Ise. The ceremonies include the dedication of textiles and sacred dances known as kagura. <ref>https://www.jinjahoncho.or.jp/en/shinto/index6.html</ref>

Today, many shrines across Japan observe Tsukinami-no-Matsuri by designating a specific day each month to conduct the festival, maintaining the tradition of offering prayers and performing rituals to honor the deities and seek their blessings. <ref name=":0" />

== history ==
It seems that in the past it was held every month, but {{ill|Engishiki|the Engishiki|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki|qq=}} stipulates that it should be held on the 11th of June and December, and it was held at {{ill|10=ja|11=朝廷_(日本)|Imperial Court in Kyoto|lt=the Imperial Court|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Imperial Court of Japan|WD=Q30253177}} and at Ise Shrine.

On the morning of the 11th day, {{ill|Department of Divinities|lt=the Shinto priests|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Department of Divinities|qq=}} at the Imperial Court distributed {{ill|10=ja|11=幣帛|hanpei|lt=offerings|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} (hanpei) to the 304 {{ill|10=ja|11=祝_(神職)|Kami|lt=Kami|lt=Deity|lt=deities|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} of {{ill|Kansai region|the Kinai region|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansai region|qq=}} <ref name="yougo">{{Cite book|title=日本史B用語集―A併記|last=全国歴史教育研究協議会|publisher={{ill|山川出版社|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/山川出版社|qq=}}|isbn=9784634013025|edition=改訂版|series=|author-link=|access-date=|和書|qq=draft}}</ref> . In the evening, a ceremony called "jinkonjiki" was held at the Shinkaden Hall of {{ill|10=ja|11=中和院|Chukain Temple|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}}, in which the emperor and the gods ate grains from the previous year (old grains) <ref name="yougo" /> .

After that, handicraft offerings were only practiced at Ise Shrine, and during {{ill|Muromachi period|8=en|lt=the Muromachi period,|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi period|qq=}} handicraft offerings were abolished due to events such as {{ill|Ōnin War|lt=the Onin War|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnin War|qq=}}, but were revived after {{ill|Meiji period|lt=the Meiji period|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji period|qq=}} . Currently, many shrines across the country hold monthly festivals on specific days each month.
== Shinto Shrine of Shusse Inari in America ==
The Shinto Shrine of Shusse Inari in America, based in Los Angeles, holds monthly Tsukinami-sai (月次祭) ceremonies to honor and express gratitude to the nature spirits, or Kami-sama. These ceremonies are typically live-streamed on their official YouTube channel, "ShintoInari," allowing participants from various locations to join. <ref>https://shintoinari.org/202412-tsukinami/</ref>

For December 2024, the Tsukinami-sai was held on Sunday, December 1st, at 7 PM Pacific Time. The ceremony included expressions of respect, gratitude, and prayers for blessings from the Kami-sama, with a closing talk titled "Living with Kami-sama in Daily Life." The shrine encourages participants to follow traditional customs, such as offering Osaisen (donations), which support the continuation of these ceremonies and their goal of establishing a physical shrine in Los Angeles. <ref>https://shintoinari.org/202412-tsukinami/</ref>

Looking ahead, the shrine has scheduled the Saitan-sai (歳旦祭), a New Year's ceremony, on January 1st, 2024, at 11:00 AM Pacific Time. This event will be held at a pop-up shrine in Little Tokyo's Weller Court, Los Angeles, and will also be live-streamed for online participants. Following this, the first Tsukinami-sai of 2024 is planned for the same day at 3:00 PM Pacific Time, both in-person and online. <ref>https://shintoinari.org/2024saitan-202401tsukinami/</ref>

For the most current information on upcoming Tsukinami-sai ceremonies and other events, it's advisable to visit the Shinto Shrine of Shusse Inari in America's official website or their YouTube channel. <ref>https://www.youtube.com/%40shusseinarishrineofamerica1913</ref>

== history == It seems that in the past it was held every month, but {{ill|Engishiki|the Engishiki|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki|qq=}} stipulates that it should be held on the 11th of June and December, and it was held at {{ill|10=ja|11=朝廷_(日本)|Imperial Court in Kyoto|lt=the Imperial Court|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Imperial Court of Japan|WD=Q30253177}} and at Ise Shrine.
On the morning of the 11th day, {{ill|Department of Divinities|lt=the Shinto priests|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Department of Divinities|qq=}} at the Imperial Court distributed {{ill|10=ja|11=幣帛|hanpei|lt=offerings|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} (hanpei) to the 304 {{ill|10=ja|11=祝_(神職)|Kami|lt=Kami|lt=Deity|lt=deities|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} of {{ill|Kansai region|the Kinai region|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansai region|qq=}} <ref name="yougo">{{Cite book|title=日本史B用語集―A併記|last=全国歴史教育研究協議会|publisher={{ill|山川出版社|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/山川出版社|qq=}}|isbn=9784634013025|edition=改訂版|series=|author-link=|access-date=|和書|qq=draft}}</ref> . In the evening, a ceremony called "jinkonjiki" was held at the Shinkaden Hall of {{ill|10=ja|11=中和院|Chukain Temple|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}}, in which the emperor and the gods ate grains from the previous year (old grains) <ref name="yougo" /> .
After that, handicraft offerings were only practiced at Ise Shrine, and during {{ill|Muromachi period|8=en|lt=the Muromachi period,|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi period|qq=}} handicraft offerings were abolished due to events such as {{ill|Ōnin War|lt=the Onin War|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnin War|qq=}}, but were revived after {{ill|Meiji period|lt=the Meiji period|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji period|qq=}} . Currently, many shrines across the country hold monthly festivals on specific days each month. == Shinto Shrine of Shusse Inari in America == The Shinto Shrine of Shusse Inari in America, based in Los Angeles, holds monthly Tsukinami-sai (月次祭) ceremonies to honor and express gratitude to the nature spirits, or Kami-sama. These ceremonies are typically live-streamed on their official YouTube channel, "ShintoInari," allowing participants from various locations to join. <ref>https://shintoinari.org/202412-tsukinami/</ref>
For December 2024, the Tsukinami-sai was held on Sunday, December 1st, at 7 PM Pacific Time. The ceremony included expressions of respect, gratitude, and prayers for blessings from the Kami-sama, with a closing talk titled "Living with Kami-sama in Daily Life." The shrine encourages participants to follow traditional customs, such as offering Osaisen (donations), which support the continuation of these ceremonies and their goal of establishing a physical shrine in Los Angeles. <ref>https://shintoinari.org/202412-tsukinami/</ref>
Looking ahead, the shrine has scheduled the Saitan-sai (歳旦祭), a New Year's ceremony, on January 1st, 2024, at 11:00 AM Pacific Time. This event will be held at a pop-up shrine in Little Tokyo's Weller Court, Los Angeles, and will also be live-streamed for online participants. Following this, the first Tsukinami-sai of 2024 is planned for the same day at 3:00 PM Pacific Time, both in-person and online. <ref>https://shintoinari.org/2024saitan-202401tsukinami/</ref>
For the most current information on upcoming Tsukinami-sai ceremonies and other events, it's advisable to visit the Shinto Shrine of Shusse Inari in America's official website or their YouTube channel. <ref>https://www.youtube.com/%40shusseinarishrineofamerica1913</ref>

'''Tsukinami no Matsuri''' is one of the {{ill|Shinto|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto|qq=}} {{ill|Festival|lt=festivals|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Festival|qq=}} . At {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Shrine|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Grand Shrine|qq=}}, the three festivals held in {{ill|June|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/June|qq=}} and {{ill|December|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/December|qq=}} and the [[:en:Kannamesai Festival|Kannamesai Festival]] are called the Sansetsu Festival and Sanjisai Festival.
Tsukinami-no-Matsuri, meaning "monthly festival," is a significant Shinto ritual traditionally observed at the Ise Grand Shrine in Japan. Despite its name, the festival is held biannually, on the 11th day of June and December. It is one of the Sansetsu-sai festivals, along with the Kanname-sai festival in October, collectively known as the three major festivals of Ise Shrine. <ref name=":0">https://www.japanesewiki.com/Shinto/Tsukinami-no-Matsuri.html</ref>
Historically, during Tsukinami-no-Matsuri, the Imperial Court would present offerings, known as heihaku (silk offerings), to 304 deities worshipped in the Kinai region. This practice, referred to as hanhei, was later conducted exclusively by the Ise Grand Shrine. Although hanhei was discontinued during the Muromachi period due to conflicts like the Ōnin War, it was reinstated in the Meiji period. <ref name=":0" />
The festival involves solemn rituals and ceremonies conducted by Shinto priests, who offer prayers for peace and prosperity. These rituals are performed under the direction of the Emperor, considered a direct descendant of Amaterasu-Omikami, the sun goddess enshrined at Ise. The ceremonies include the dedication of textiles and sacred dances known as kagura. <ref>https://www.jinjahoncho.or.jp/en/shinto/index6.html</ref>
Today, many shrines across Japan observe Tsukinami-no-Matsuri by designating a specific day each month to conduct the festival, maintaining the tradition of offering prayers and performing rituals to honor the deities and seek their blessings. <ref name=":0" />
== history == It seems that in the past it was held every month, but {{ill|Engishiki|the Engishiki|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki|qq=}} stipulates that it should be held on the 11th of June and December, and it was held at {{ill|10=ja|11=朝廷_(日本)|Imperial Court in Kyoto|lt=the Imperial Court|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Imperial Court of Japan|WD=Q30253177}} and at Ise Shrine.
On the morning of the 11th day, {{ill|Department of Divinities|lt=the Shinto priests|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Department of Divinities|qq=}} at the Imperial Court distributed {{ill|10=ja|11=幣帛|hanpei|lt=offerings|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} (hanpei) to the 304 {{ill|10=ja|11=祝_(神職)|Kami|lt=Kami|lt=Deity|lt=deities|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} of {{ill|Kansai region|the Kinai region|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansai region|qq=}} <ref name="yougo">{{Cite book|title=日本史B用語集―A併記|last=全国歴史教育研究協議会|publisher={{ill|山川出版社|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/山川出版社|qq=}}|isbn=9784634013025|edition=改訂版|series=|author-link=|access-date=|和書|qq=draft}}</ref> . In the evening, a ceremony called "jinkonjiki" was held at the Shinkaden Hall of {{ill|10=ja|11=中和院|Chukain Temple|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}}, in which the emperor and the gods ate grains from the previous year (old grains) <ref name="yougo" /> .
After that, handicraft offerings were only practiced at Ise Shrine, and during {{ill|Muromachi period|8=en|lt=the Muromachi period,|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi period|qq=}} handicraft offerings were abolished due to events such as {{ill|Ōnin War|lt=the Onin War|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnin War|qq=}}, but were revived after {{ill|Meiji period|lt=the Meiji period|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji period|qq=}} . Currently, many shrines across the country hold monthly festivals on specific days each month. == Shinto Shrine of Shusse Inari in America == The Shinto Shrine of Shusse Inari in America, based in Los Angeles, holds monthly Tsukinami-sai (月次祭) ceremonies to honor and express gratitude to the nature spirits, or Kami-sama. These ceremonies are typically live-streamed on their official YouTube channel, "ShintoInari," allowing participants from various locations to join. <ref>https://shintoinari.org/202412-tsukinami/</ref>
For December 2024, the Tsukinami-sai was held on Sunday, December 1st, at 7 PM Pacific Time. The ceremony included expressions of respect, gratitude, and prayers for blessings from the Kami-sama, with a closing talk titled "Living with Kami-sama in Daily Life." The shrine encourages participants to follow traditional customs, such as offering Osaisen (donations), which support the continuation of these ceremonies and their goal of establishing a physical shrine in Los Angeles. <ref>https://shintoinari.org/202412-tsukinami/</ref>
Looking ahead, the shrine has scheduled the Saitan-sai (歳旦祭), a New Year's ceremony, on January 1st, 2024, at 11:00 AM Pacific Time. This event will be held at a pop-up shrine in Little Tokyo's Weller Court, Los Angeles, and will also be live-streamed for online participants. Following this, the first Tsukinami-sai of 2024 is planned for the same day at 3:00 PM Pacific Time, both in-person and online. <ref>https://shintoinari.org/2024saitan-202401tsukinami/</ref>
For the most current information on upcoming Tsukinami-sai ceremonies and other events, it's advisable to visit the Shinto Shrine of Shusse Inari in America's official website or their YouTube channel. <ref>https://www.youtube.com/%40shusseinarishrineofamerica1913</ref>

== Shinto Shrine of Shusse Inari in America ==
The Shinto Shrine of Shusse Inari in America, based in Los Angeles, holds monthly Tsukinami-sai (月次祭) ceremonies to honor and express gratitude to the nature spirits, or Kami-sama. These ceremonies are typically live-streamed on their official YouTube channel, "ShintoInari," allowing participants from various locations to join. <ref>https://shintoinari.org/202412-tsukinami/</ref>

For December 2024, the Tsukinami-sai was held on Sunday, December 1st, at 7 PM Pacific Time. The ceremony included expressions of respect, gratitude, and prayers for blessings from the Kami-sama, with a closing talk titled "Living with Kami-sama in Daily Life." The shrine encourages participants to follow traditional customs, such as offering Osaisen (donations), which support the continuation of these ceremonies and their goal of establishing a physical shrine in Los Angeles. <ref>https://shintoinari.org/202412-tsukinami/</ref>

Looking ahead, the shrine has scheduled the Saitan-sai (歳旦祭), a New Year's ceremony, on January 1st, 2024, at 11:00 AM Pacific Time. This event will be held at a pop-up shrine in Little Tokyo's Weller Court, Los Angeles, and will also be live-streamed for online participants. Following this, the first Tsukinami-sai of 2024 is planned for the same day at 3:00 PM Pacific Time, both in-person and online. <ref>https://shintoinari.org/2024saitan-202401tsukinami/</ref>

For the most current information on upcoming Tsukinami-sai ceremonies and other events, it's advisable to visit the Shinto Shrine of Shusse Inari in America's official website or their YouTube channel. <ref>https://www.youtube.com/%40shusseinarishrineofamerica1913</ref>
